title: Stitched Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam description provides strong gameplay evidence: the game explicitly centers on turn-based battles, leveling up, gaining experience, growing bonds to unlock new abilities, traveling with a friend party, and using pair-specific Link Skills in combat. It also emphasizes an authored story about saving the world, explorable locations across two connected worlds, and environmental puzzles tied to world-switching. That combination strongly fits a story-forward JRPG structure with party progression and turn-based tactical encounters. Secondary CRPG/tactical signals exist from party management and puzzle-solving, but the strongest identity is JRPG-style story RPG rather than pure tactics or point-and-click adventure.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally solid for a story-driven party JRPG, especially where candidates combine turn-based combat with exploration and puzzle-solving. Best fits capture the anchor's authored adventure, ensemble party dynamics, and between-battle traversal; weaker fits drift toward social-sim structure or rely more on tonal/franchise reputation than evidenced gameplay overlap.
anchor_summary: Story-forward single-player indie JRPG: party-based turn-based combat, character progression and bond-linked abilities, authored mystery campaign, explorable spaces with environmental puzzle solving, and dual-world traversal between ordinary and alternate realities.
gap_notes: The strongest missing throughline in some candidates is the anchor's explicit environmental puzzle-solving and world-manipulation loop between battles.
Several candidates match on narrative tone or party friendship, but the anchor is specifically a turn-based exploration RPG rather than a pure social-sim or purely reputation-based classic comp.
Evidence quality is uneven: some candidates have sparse stored text, making confidence lower even when the title is intuitively relevant.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit on small-party authored JRPG structure, turn-based battles, character ability synergy, and frequent exploration/puzzle traversal between fights. Slightly less aligned on mystery/bond-social progression specifics.

Matches turn-based party RPG combat, teen ensemble, strong story focus, and substantial exploration with hidden treasure and puzzle-like traversal. More tactical/grid-leaning and less dual-world/mystery-driven than the anchor.

Good overlap on story-first indie RPG, party combat, emotional bonds, and movement between mundane and strange worlds. Less clear evidence for puzzle-heavy overworld structure and party-link mechanical customization.

Shares mystery campaign, close friend-group dynamics, turn-based battles, and relationship progression affecting combat. Its calendar/social-sim and dungeon routine is a more distinct structure than the anchor's exploration-puzzle JRPG flow.

A plausible heritage comp for eccentric modern-setting party JRPGs, but provided evidence is minimal and the anchor's explicit puzzle/world-switching and bond-based combat emphasis is not demonstrated here.

title: Dragonkin: The Banished

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present an action-RPG loop centered on choosing one of four hero classes, fighting hordes of enemies and dragon bosses, acquiring skill loot, optimizing builds through the Ancestral Grid, evolving character and gear, and continuing into endgame activities. The game also emphasizes up-to-4-player co-op across the full adventure and a hub-city upgrade layer, which fits a co-op-capable loot-driven action RPG more than any match-based or shooter taxonomy result suggested by the noisy computed near-misses. Evidence is strong for dominant combat, class/build differentiation, gear/skill progression, and replayable campaign structure, but insufficient to lock perspective or exact world topology more precisely.

overall_note: The candidate set is directionally strong for a loot-driven co-op action RPG rail. The best fits emphasize class-based hack-and-slash combat, enemy swarms, loot/build progression, and replayable campaign-to-endgame loops. The main gaps are lack of a truly near-identical comp with the same city-building layer and some candidates being more solo/buildcraft-oriented or represented by an expansion entry rather than the cleaner base-game comp.
anchor_summary: Dragonkin: The Banished is best understood as a co-op-capable loot action RPG: class-based hero selection, horde-clearing combat, gear/skill loot, deep build progression via the Ancestral Grid, full-campaign co-op, and endgame continuation, with a hub-city upgrade layer as secondary structure.
gap_notes: No candidate closely mirrors the anchor's hub-city development/shared town progression layer; most comps match combat/progression better than meta-structure.
Several candidates fit the loot ARPG identity well, but some lean more toward solo-first or broader MMO/shared-world structure than the anchor's focused 1-4 player co-op adventure.
Diablo III is represented by Reaper of Souls rather than the base game, which is slightly awkward for rail clarity even though the gameplay fit is strong.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: 4-player hack-and-slash ARPG with distinct classes, horde combat, loot progression, and campaign/endgame co-op structure.
Premier loot ARPG benchmark with class builds, co-op play, dungeon/boss farming, and extensive endgame progression; shared-world/PvP elements make it a bit broader than the anchor.
Fast co-op loot ARPG with strong class identity, massive enemy packs, gear chase, and repeatable endgame loops; excellent gameplay comp despite being an expansion listing.
Accessible class-based loot ARPG with co-op, randomized areas, and continuous build development, but lighter in endgame sophistication and overall structure.
Excellent match on buildcraft, skill specialization, dungeon crawling, and loot progression, but comparatively more solo/build-focused and less defined by 4-player campaign co-op identity.

title: Starship Troopers: Ultimate Bug War!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as an 'action-packed, first-person retro shooter' set in the Starship Troopers universe, with the player joining the Mobile Infantry against the Arachnids. The Steam detailed copy adds key structure: a solo campaign 'packed with missions, secrets,' a large weapon arsenal, and 'epic battlefields,' which supports a campaign-driven first-person shooter identity rather than a match-based arena FPS. The Mobile Infantry framing, battlefield combat, and anti-Bug arsenal support a military-FPS classification despite the sci-fi setting.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally solid for a retro/boomer-shooter campaign FPS, with the best candidates matching the anchor’s first-person gunplay, stage-based solo structure, secrets, and heavy arsenal. The main gap is that none are especially close on the anchor’s military-sci-fi squad-war framing against insect hordes, so the rail leans more toward broad retro FPS identity than a truly specific Starship Troopers-like comp set.
anchor_summary: A single-player retro-styled first-person shooter built around a campaign of combat-heavy missions, secret-hunting, and a large weapon arsenal, with high-action sci-fi military combat against swarming alien enemies.
gap_notes: No candidate directly matches the anchor’s military-sci-fi campaign framing with Mobile Infantry-style anti-bug warfare.
The strongest fits come from retro FPS structure and weapon-driven solo campaigns rather than exact enemy/challenge identity.
Several candidates add significant multiplayer/editor components that are not central to the anchor’s profile.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on modern retro-FPS identity: fast first-person gunplay, chunky sci-fi arsenal, large combat spaces, secrets, and a linear solo campaign.
Excellent boomer-shooter match on aggressive movement, gore, heavy weapons, and hand-crafted campaign maps with hidden areas, though its broader mode set is less aligned.
Strong fit on high-action FPS combat, oversized arsenal, and mowing down huge swarms through campaign missions, but it is more arcade-horde focused than retro secret-driven.
Good match for classic solo FPS structure, key-hunting, secrets, and run-and-gun combat, but its Build-style exploratory level design is a bit more maze-forward than the anchor suggests.

A valid foundational retro FPS comparison for mission-map progression, weapon-driven combat, and secret discovery, but it is a broader classic benchmark than a particularly specific match for this anchor.

title: Bonnie Bear Saves Frogtime

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a comedy adventure built around 'tactical frog battles' where players 'collect, buy and trade frogs' to 'build an unbeatable roster' while embarking on quests and exploring an absurd world. That strongly supports a creature-collection RPG identity with tactical party-based battles, with tactical_rpg as a secondary read. Perspective, exact battle format, and setting are not clearly stated, so those fields remain conservative.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor’s monster-collection plus tactical/turn-based party-building core reasonably well, but most candidates lean more toward conventional monster-taming RPGs than the anchor’s specifically tactical-battler framing.
anchor_summary: Comedy adventure RPG centered on collecting, buying, and trading frogs to build a roster for tactical battles, with overworld questing and progression driven by creature-team optimization in a single-player campaign.
gap_notes: No candidate is an especially direct match for a whimsical single-player adventure where nearly every conflict is resolved through a dedicated tactical frog-battle system.
The rail skews toward broader monster-collect RPG comps; stronger fits would more consistently emphasize tactical battle structure rather than standard JRPG-style encounters.
One candidate adds substantial side-scrolling/metroidvania platforming, which makes it more structurally adjacent than directly similar.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on creature collection, roster-building, exploration, quests, and turn-based battling; a strong audience fit even if its battle format appears more conventional monster RPG than explicit tactics.
Strong match on monster-taming campaign structure, squad progression, and strategic turn-based combat; less distinctive on adventure-comedy tone and not clearly as tactics-forward as the anchor.
Shares the core catch/build/battle adventure loop and open exploration, but it is a broader mainstream monster RPG comp rather than a close match for the anchor’s tactical battler identity.
Good overlap in collectible team-building and strategic monster combat, but the side-scrolling metroidvania structure and platforming make it notably looser than the other candidates.

title: Maestro

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Maestro as a VR orchestra conducting rhythm game. The core loop is timing- and gesture-driven: players use hand tracking to keep tempo, cue instruments, and shape music in real time, which strongly supports rhythm_game over weaker near-miss candidates. The game is described as playing through tracks/concert performances rather than exploration or narrative progression, fitting match_session structure, timing_input, rhythm_timing mechanics, and the hit_beats goal. It is single-player, non-combat, and presented as a premium replayable track-based experience with unlockable stages and cosmetics. While Steam also tags it as simulation/casual, that reads as secondary framing around realistic conducting technique rather than a different core genre.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally correct: all candidates are music/VR performance neighbors, and the top half captures Maestro's rhythm-and-gesture identity well. The main weakness is that several picks drift from conducting-focused rhythm play into instrument simulation, music creation, or hybrid race/combat structures.
anchor_summary: Maestro is a single-player VR rhythm game centered on hand-tracked orchestral conducting. Its gameplay identity is expressive timing and gesture control across track-based performances, with replay, scoring/mastery, and light unlock progression rather than combat, exploration, or open-ended music creation.
gap_notes: Beat Saber and Synth Riders are the clearest comps because they share VR, song-based sessions, motion timing, replayability, and mastery.
Drums Rock and Ragnarock are understandable but less aligned because their core loop is drumming/hitting targets, with stronger arcade or hybrid framing than expressive conducting.
Virtuoso is the loosest fit: it shares VR musical performance and hand expression, but it is primarily a creative sandbox rather than a score-chasing rhythm game.
No candidate is a direct base-game/DLC pairing or near-identical conducting comp, so the rail lacks a true must-keep benchmark beyond flagship VR rhythm neighbors.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Flagship VR rhythm game with motion-controlled, track-based precision play, high replay value, and similar mastery expectations; less expressive/conducting-focused, but still the clearest audience overlap.
Very close on VR rhythm identity, full-body timing input, song sessions, and performance flow. More note-lane/dance driven than Maestro, but strongly aligned on core loop.
VR music-performance rhythm game with physical instrument play, track progression, and skill mastery. The demon-smashing arcade campaign and drum-kit focus make it a looser fit than the top two.

Shares VR rhythm sessions, gesture timing, score chasing, and replayability, but its drumming-plus-racing structure and competitive multiplayer emphasis pull it away from Maestro's conducting identity.
Shares VR musical embodiment and expressive hand-led performance, but it is mainly a music-creation sandbox rather than a structured rhythm game with comparable challenge and progression.

title: Altiros

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an online shared-world sandbox adventure with third-person exploration, building, terrain destruction, co-op/MMO-style social play, and some monster fighting. However, the core gameplay identity does not cleanly match any allowed archetype. It is not well-supported as mmo_action_rpg because progression structure, RPG build identity, and combat loop are underspecified; not open_world_action_adventure because construction/sandbox settlement play appears equally or more central than authored action-adventure structure; and not exploration_survival_adventure because survival pressures are absent. The current mapping-gap signals also show no candidate archetype reached threshold. Because the evidence is concrete but points to a gameplay blend outside the available taxonomy rather than a strong fit within it, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a mismatched archetype.

overall_note: The set captures Altiros's voxel/sandbox/social identity fairly well, with Trove, Portal Knights, Boundless, and Minecraft all understandable comparisons. The main weakness is that some candidates over-index toward survival, economy, or class/loot RPG structure relative to Altiros's more build-explore-destroy shared-world pitch.
anchor_summary: Altiros reads as a third-person online sandbox adventure centered on block building, terrain destruction, open exploration across sky islands, light real-time combat, and social/shared-world play. The best comps should preserve that blend of creative construction, traversal/exploration, multiplayer world structure, and accessible action rather than focusing only on fantasy tone or generic RPG elements.
gap_notes: No candidate is a perfect 'same' fit because Altiros appears more sandbox-building-forward and less progression-defined than the RPG/MMO-heavy options.
Portal Knights is especially valuable because it matches third-person co-op sandbox adventuring and island structure, even if it is less shared-world persistent.
Creativerse is the loosest acceptable fit due to its stronger survival/crafting pressures versus Altiros's lighter, more social sandbox framing.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest overall benchmark: colorful voxel online sandbox with building, destructible worlds, exploration, multiplayer social play, and light action combat. Trove is more class/loot-driven, but the core audience overlap is obvious.

Very strong fit on third-person co-op sandbox adventuring, block building, island-based world structure, crafting, and real-time combat. Less MMO/shared-world than Altiros, but the play feel is close.
Strong match for persistent online voxel sandbox play, settlement building, world sculpting, exploration, and social progression. It is less combat-forward and more economy/city-focused than Altiros.

Major overlap in block building, terrain manipulation, exploration, and multiplayer sandbox expression. However, Minecraft is less specifically third-person, less MMO-shaped, and less anchored to action-adventure combat structure.

Understandable sandbox comp for building, terraforming, creatures, and multiplayer exploration, but its stronger survival/crafting systems and less distinct social-action identity make it a weaker rail fit.

title: Pizza Slice

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Pizza Slice as a restaurant business simulation centered on managing a family pizzeria: shopping for ingredients, prep work, making pizzas, expanding tables, buying better kitchenware, learning recipes, keeping customers happy, and growing from a small shop into a larger franchise. Metacritic explicitly calls it a strategy/simulation game with economic elements and a pizzeria manager role. Steam categories also support single-player, co-op, and PvP, with the detailed description naming an additive team-vs-team cook-off mode; however, the dominant identity remains the core business-management sandbox rather than competitive match play. The evidence strongly fits management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue and franchise growth, not urban planning or autonomous colony management.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's restaurant-management identity reasonably well, with one especially strong pizzeria benchmark and several broader restaurant/cafe sims. The main weakness is that some candidates lean more toward arcade co-op cooking or broader hospitality management than the anchor's economic pizzeria tycoon core.
anchor_summary: Pizza Slice is primarily a restaurant/pizzeria management tycoon: a business-sim loop of ingredient purchasing, prep, pizza creation, customer satisfaction, upgrades, expansion, and franchise growth, with hands-on operational tasks and optional co-op/PvP modes as secondary features.
gap_notes: A must-have direct pizzeria business sim is present, but the rest skew broader restaurant/cafe rather than tightly matching pizzeria franchise management.
The anchor's blend of hands-on food prep and business simulation is represented, but dedicated economic/tycoon structure is stronger in some picks than others.
Co-op similarity appears in one candidate, but most of the set does not reflect the anchor's optional multiplayer/PvP components.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct pizzeria economic sim with recipe creation, restaurant expansion, competitor pressure, and rise-from-small-shop-to-chain progression; this is the clearest gameplay match.
Very close on restaurant ownership, layout, menu design, recipe systems, staffing, and sandbox business growth, though it is broader restaurant tycoon rather than specifically pizza-focused.
Strong match on running a pizza shop, fulfilling orders, upgrading equipment and toppings, and staying profitable against rivals, but lighter and narrower in management scope.

Shares hospitality tycoon fundamentals like staff management, supply chain, customer satisfaction, recipes, and business expansion, but cafe simulation is a looser thematic and operational fit than a restaurant/pizzeria sim.

It overlaps on cooking, restaurant layout, upgrades, and co-op operation, but its frantic roguelite/arcade service loop is meaningfully different from the anchor's economic business-management sandbox.

title: Mythmatch

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mythmatch as a narrative-rich town/community-building merge game focused on rebuilding Ithaca, matching items and people, relationships, and replayable god-themed minigames. However, the evidence does not strongly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a city_builder, farming_sim, life_sim, management_tycoon, or puzzle-led archetype within the available list, and the referenced minigames appear additive rather than the core identity. Provider metadata also produces weak or noisy signals (for example co-op/multiplayer categories without concrete gameplay structure, and a simulation genre tag). Because the game’s core gameplay identity appears to sit outside the current archetype set, classification into an existing archetype would be speculative.

overall_note: The rail captures Mythmatch's cozy helping-people/community-rebuilding side, but neither candidate matches the anchor's core merge-game loop particularly closely. These are usable neighbors, not highly precise gameplay comps.
anchor_summary: Mythmatch is a narrative-rich community/town-building merge game centered on matching items and people to rebuild Ithaca, with relationship-building and a few side minigame challenge modes. title: Don't Mess With Bober

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short first-person 'walking simulator with extra mechanics' focused on atmosphere, light fishing, and hide-and-seek/chase sequences with a revenge-seeking beaver. The horror framing is present, but there is not enough evidence for a supported core archetype such as survival_horror, psychological_horror, action_horror, or stealth_action_adventure: combat is not established, resource-survival structure is absent, puzzle structure is unclear, and stealth is only implied by hide-and-seek/chase wording. The available text points more strongly to a short first-person narrative/walking-sim experience, which is not an available taxonomy archetype here, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

overall_note: The set is directionally solid because all candidates share the anchor’s first-person, story-led exploration structure and low-combat profile. Best fits are compact atmospheric horror/mystery adventures; the biggest gap is that none match the anchor’s very short novelty-horror setup with explicit chase/evade interplay and light side mechanics like fishing.
anchor_summary: Short single-player first-person narrative horror/walking-sim hybrid: exploration-driven, very light mechanics, brief runtime, and tension built around hide-and-seek/chase sequences rather than combat-heavy survival systems.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches the anchor’s ultra-short 30–60 minute format.

title: Adventurous Slime

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'mini metroidvania.' The gameplay copy supports that label with side-scrolling platforming, alternative paths, exploration across 4 biomes, dangerous creatures, powerful ancient items, boss fights, and a simplified experience system. Visual presentation is clearly 2D pixel art, and the game is single-player with a compact but authored campaign structure. While some combat specifics are not deeply detailed, the core exploration-gating-plus-bosses structure is strong enough to classify it as metroidvania.

overall_note: The candidate set is directionally solid: most picks are genuine 2D exploration platformers or metroidvanias, and several match the anchor's compact, accessible feel well. The best fits are shorter, friendlier metroidvanias with clear upgrade gating and modest scope. A few candidates skew meaningfully more combat- or challenge-driven than the anchor, but there are no outright wrong inclusions.
anchor_summary: Adventurous Slime is a short, approachable 2D single-player metroidvania: side-scrolling exploration across a small authored world with alternative paths, item/ability-style progression, bosses, light RPG elements, and a breezy mini-campaign rather than a large or highly punishing challenge game.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are compact and approachable metroidvanias; candidates that emphasize intense combat and reflex challenge drift away from the anchor's casual mini-metroidvania identity.
Several candidates fit the structure well but differ in combat model, with shooter-leaning or harder action focus compared with the anchor's lighter, simplified setup.
No obvious base-game/DLC pairing exists here, so the rail depends on strong structural neighbors rather than essential franchise adjacency.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close structural match: short 2D exploration-platforming with power-up gating, secrets, bosses, and a concise metroidvania campaign. More shooter-focused than the anchor, but scope and loop align very well.

A friendly, compact metroidvania with upgrade-gated exploration, secrets, bosses, and a breezier tone/challenge level that suits the anchor well. Slightly larger and more traversal-polished, but a strong audience fit.

Compact handcrafted metroidvania with map exploration, item unlocks, secrets, and bosses. The mech/cat split adds stronger ability-expression and combat identity, but it remains a very close genre and scope match.

Interconnected exploration-platforming with transformation-based gating and adjustable difficulty makes it a sensible comp. It is more classic action-adventure/platformer in feel than a modern mini-metroidvania, so the fit is a bit looser.

Clearly overlaps on side-scrolling interconnected exploration, ability gating, and bosses, but it leans more into tight combat, reflex challenges, and a harsher action profile than the anchor's lighter casual mini-metroidvania style.

title: Subsequence

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently support a short, single-player, third-person, story-driven exploration adventure with light logic puzzles, item use, collectibles, and humor in a sci-fi facility setting. However, that evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is explicitly not puzzle-heavy, does not show clear platforming emphasis, does not establish meaningful combat loops, and lacks the stronger authored action/setpiece profile needed for cinematic_action_adventure or the horror/combat grounding needed for action_horror, survival_horror, or psychological_horror. This appears closer to a light narrative exploration adventure/walking-adventure hybrid, which is not an available archetype in the allowed list.

overall_note: The rail understands the anchor as a compact story-led exploration game with light puzzle friction. Best fits are other short narrative exploration adventures, especially sci-fi mystery spaces. The main gaps are perspective mismatch across several candidates and a few picks that lean more toward platforming or action than the anchor does.
anchor_summary: Subsequence is a short, self-contained single-player third-person narrative exploration adventure: light logic puzzles, simple item use, collectibles, minimal controls, and story-first progression through a sci-fi facility with a relaxed tone and only light tension.
gap_notes: No candidate is a truly near-identical third-person, short-form, light-puzzle sci-fi facility adventure; most good matches are first-person narrative exploration games.
The set overreaches a bit toward action/platforming with The Gunk and, to a lesser extent, Lifeless Moon, while Subsequence appears more guided and lower-intensity.
The strongest shared identity is compact authored exploration and narrative discovery, not combat, challenge, or systems depth.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very strong match on short sci-fi facility exploration, low mechanical complexity, environmental storytelling, and narrative-first progression; weaker only because it is first-person and even less puzzle/item-driven.
Shares trapped-in-complex setup, authored progression, exploration, and puzzle-gated escape, but it is first-person and notably more puzzle-centric with a stronger bespoke mechanic.

Excellent fit on short self-contained story-led exploration with simple interactions, but the anthology structure, first-person presentation, and very different tone/world model make it a looser gameplay comp.

Third-person sci-fi exploration and light puzzle solving line up well, but it leans more into traversal/platforming and broader environmental journeying than Subsequence’s guided narrative-facility structure.

Third-person accessible sci-fi exploration helps, but its action-adventure loop, tool use, environmental cleansing, and combat/reactivity push it away from Subsequence’s low-intensity narrative adventure identity.

title: Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently frames this as an RPG in the Monster Hunter Stories series where the player 'become[s] a Rider,' 'rais[es] and bond[s] with your favorite monsters,' and 'explore[s] the vibrant world of Monster Hunter.' Those are strong identity signals for a monster-collect RPG, especially the explicit monster bonding/raising loop. The narrative copy also emphasizes a story-led journey across countries with a Rider and trusted Rathalos, supporting a campaign-driven heroic adventure structure. Evidence for exact perspective, combat format, and more detailed structural facets is limited, so those fields are left blank rather than inferred too aggressively.

overall_note: The rail has the two obvious series benchmarks and then mostly reasonable monster-collecting JRPG neighbors. It weakens toward the bottom because some picks share creature collection and turn-based combat but miss the Rider/exploration/raising identity or have more distinct battle/progression structures.
anchor_summary: Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection appears to be a story-driven monster-collecting JRPG built around becoming a Rider, bonding/raising monsters, exploration, egg collection/hatching, and turn-based party battles. Best matches should share that specific collectible-creature RPG loop more than just broad JRPG overlap.
gap_notes: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin and Monster Hunter Stories are essential series comps and should lead the rail.
The middle of the rail is solidly monster-collect RPG adjacent, but there is a noticeable drop after the direct series entries.
Shin Megami Tensei V: Vengeance fits on collectible-creature party building and turn-based combat, but demon recruitment/fusion and harsher progression give it a different gameplay feel.

World of Final Fantasy is a fair lower-end inclusion, but its stacking system and more linear, gimmick-driven structure make it less representative of the anchor's core loop than the stronger candidates.

title: Rhell: Warped Worlds & Troubled Times

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a semi-open world puzzle adventure centered on combining magical runes to create spells, exploring interconnected areas, solving environmental puzzles with multiple solutions, and pursuing a branching mystery narrative. That evidence is strong for a systemic puzzle-adventure identity, but the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly cover this profile. The strongest supported traits are semi-open exploration, environmental puzzle solving, authored branching narrative, and systemic spell-based problem solving; combat is not described as a primary loop, and the evidence does not firmly establish a supported archetype such as open_world_action_adventure, western_narrative_rpg, or immersive_sim_action. Because the game's core gameplay identity falls between taxonomy options and forcing a combat- or RPG-led archetype would misclassify it, rejection is more appropriate than a weak accept.

overall_note: The set has several credible neighbors for systemic exploration-puzzle play, but it mixes strong gameplay comps with looser mystery/narrative-adventure matches. Best candidates emphasize ability combination, exploration gating, and environment-first problem solving rather than combat or pure narrative deduction.
anchor_summary: Semi-open world puzzle adventure built around combining magical runes into systemic spells, using those combinations for multi-solution environmental problem solving, exploration of interconnected areas, and a mystery-driven branching narrative. Combat does not appear to be the main identity driver.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest when it focuses on systemic puzzle-solving and ability-combo exploration; some candidates skew too far toward narrative mystery or broad action-adventure.

The anchor appears less combat-led than several candidates, so combat-heavy open-world action games should be ranked below puzzle-forward exploration comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Directly overlaps on rune-based spell crafting, combining magic effects, and using custom spells for environmental puzzles and traversal; more combat/boss-oriented and less branching-narrative-driven than the anchor.
Very close on exploration-led puzzle play, versatile abilities with many uses, and a large interconnected world gated by new powers; lighter on story, dialogue, and branching outcomes.

Best candidates emphasize ability combination, exploration gating, and environment-first problem solving rather than combat or pure narrative deduction.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest when it focuses on systemic puzzle-solving and ability-combo exploration; some candidates skew too far toward narrative mystery or broad action-adventure.
No candidate fully matches the anchor's specific mix of spell-combination sandbox plus branching character-driven narrative, so even the best fits are neighbors rather than near-identical matches.
The anchor appears less combat-led than several candidates, so combat-heavy open-world action games should be ranked below puzzle-forward exploration comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Directly overlaps on rune-based spell crafting, combining magic effects, and using custom spells for environmental puzzles and traversal; more combat/boss-oriented and less branching-narrative-driven than the anchor.
Very close on exploration-led puzzle play, versatile abilities with many uses, and a large interconnected world gated by new powers; lighter on story, dialogue, and branching outcomes.

Excellent for open-ended systemic problem solving, traversal freedom, and multiple-solution environmental challenges, but it is much more combat-driven and broader open-world action-adventure than the anchor's puzzle-first identity.
Strong overlap in mystery, exploration, dialogue choices, side-character involvement, and multiple endings, but its core loop is deduction and conversation rather than sandbox spell experimentation.

Good match for world-as-puzzle exploration and uncovering a mystery through interconnected locations, but it lacks the anchor's spell-combination systems, branching character questing, and authored multi-solution puzzle sandbox.

title: Parkour Labs

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Parkour Labs as a movement-centric 3D parkour/platforming game built around speed, flow, precision, chaining techniques, discovering shortcuts, and unlocking new challenges across stylized surreal environments. The gameplay identity is strongly non-combat and mastery-driven, which aligns better with precision_platformer than action_platformer. Level/route unlocking and secret discovery provide a lighter secondary resemblance to 3d_collectathon, but the core loop is skillful traversal rather than collection. Multiplayer/PvP is supported by store metadata, though the main descriptive copy emphasizes single-player challenge runs and mastery. VR support introduces a first-person play option, while the broader labeling and non-VR presentation support a third-person 3D platformer identity overall.

overall_note: The rail has several solid movement-platforming comps, especially around speed, freerunning, and mastery-driven traversal. Best matches are games built around timed or challenge-based movement runs; weaker picks drift toward narrative adventure or low-pressure exploration rather than skill-test parkour structure.
anchor_summary: Parkour Labs is a movement-first 3D/first-person parkour platformer centered on speed, flow, precision, shortcut discovery, and replayable level-based skill challenges, with no real combat emphasis and optional VR support.
gap_notes: The strongest shared identity is precision/momentum platforming, not collectathon structure.
Candidates with direct time-trial or level-challenge traversal loops fit better than story-led or open-ended exploration platformers.
VR-aware movement comps are especially valuable here because the anchor explicitly supports VR.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent fit: VR-native traversal/platforming focused on freedom of movement, level challenges, fastest-time play, and skill mastery. Very closely matches the anchor’s parkour/flow identity.
Very close on fast 3D parkour, momentum chaining, difficult level-based runs, and retrowave style. Combat tools and grappling are more central than in the anchor, but traversal still dominates.
Strong match on first-person speed-platforming, replayable challenge maps, and mastery-through-time-improvement. More aggressive and arcade-hardcore, with more overt attack mechanics than the anchor.
Good non-combat traversal comp with running, jumping, sliding, and climbing in first person. Much more peaceful, short, and pressure-free, so it only partially matches the anchor’s skill-test loop.

Shared first-person traversal and momentum via grappling make it understandable, but it is more narrative-adventure focused and less built around repeatable precision challenge runs or parkour mastery.

title: Stellar Wanderer DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player open-world space combat simulator with spaceship dogfights, trading, mining, mission-taking, ship customization, and exploration across star systems. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a space-combat-sim / open-galaxy trader-pilot archetype, and the title does not cleanly fit any available accepted archetype better than a forced mismatch. Rejecting because the gameplay identity is strong but outside the supported archetype set.

overall_note: The proposed rail captures the anchor's spaceship combat and exploration well. Rebel Galaxy Outlaw is a very close match to the anchor's freelancer-style loop, while EVERSPACE 2 is a solid but more action-looter interpretation of similar spaceflight combat and progression.

title: Ultra Bonk Survivors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a fast-paced 'survivor-style action roguelite' built around quick replayable runs, endless monster waves, hero unlocks, random power-ups, and build synergies in fantasy arenas. Metacritic also labels it a third-person shooter, but the descriptive copy emphasizes a broader survivor/bullet-heaven style action loop rather than any supported taxonomy archetype here. The closest allowed archetype would be co_op_action_roguelite, but there is no evidence of co-op. Shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter are not well supported by the actual loop description. Because the game's apparent core identity does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype and forcing a fit would be misleading, rejection is appropriate.

overall_note: The candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's apparent survivors-style run structure and progression loop. The top three are very strong fits; Brotato is slightly looser due to its discrete wave/shop cadence and stronger arena-shooter feel, but still clearly relevant.
anchor_summary: Ultra Bonk Survivors appears to be a survivors-like action roguelite: short replayable arena runs, escalating enemy waves, hero unlocks, random power-ups, build synergies, and light battlefield interaction in a fantasy setting. Despite a third-person shooter label, the described core loop aligns much more with horde-survival/bullet-heaven structure than with conventional shooter archetypes.
gap_notes: No obvious bad candidates; all four share the anchor's run-based horde-survival roguelite identity.
The main uncertainty is the anchor's exact camera/control model, but the survivors-like core loop outweighs that ambiguity.
A candidate with explicit third-person over-the-shoulder action was not provided, but the anchor text does not strongly support prioritizing that anyway.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest benchmark for the anchor's survivors-like identity: short runs, endless hordes, meta-upgrades, character progression, and snowballing build synergies.

title: Anarchy Road

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text gives concrete gameplay, not just genre tags: it explicitly calls Anarchy Road a 'Post-Apocalyptic action driving game' where four heroes 'race against the clock,' 'battle bosses,' 'blast enemies,' and 'dodge traps.' Combined with racing/sports labels and co-op/split-screen categories, this supports an arcade-racer identity with strong vehicular combat and boss-focused setpiece encounters rather than a realistic racer or party game.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong because every candidate involves arcade-style vehicular racing/combat, but the best matches are the ones that capture Anarchy Road’s action-driving blend rather than pure competitive party racing. A few candidates over-index on PvP track racing or party structure versus the anchor’s boss/PvE/co-op framing.
anchor_summary: Anarchy Road looks like an arcade combat racer/action-driving game: short race-like runs with cars, hazards, shooting, boss encounters, and local/drop-in co-op support. The closest fits should emphasize vehicular combat inside an arcade racing loop, with some credit for PvE campaign structure and couch co-op, but not for post-apocalyptic theme alone.
gap_notes: The rail leans heavily toward competitive combat racers; the anchor appears to have a stronger PvE campaign/boss-run identity than most of the set.
Local co-op and same-screen play are represented well, but direct comps for race-against-the-clock plus boss encounters are limited.
No candidate is a near-identical 'same' match; most are strong or adjacent neighbors within combat racing.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best match for action-driving structure: vehicular combat, campaign progression, checkpoints/challenges, upgrades, co-op splitscreen, and explicit boss-like PvE adventure framing.

title: Solateria

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text strongly and repeatedly identify Solateria as an 'action Metroidvania' with hand-drawn side-scrolling exploration, hidden areas unlocked through skills, shortcuts, free-order world exploration, and boss-focused parry combat. The core loop centers on traversing an interconnected world, acquiring new abilities/upgrades, uncovering secrets, and defeating powerful bosses, which cleanly fits metroidvania over RPG alternatives. The parry timing, difficulty tuning, and pattern-learning boss fights also support a soulslike-flavored secondary reading, but the dominant identity is exploration-platforming progression in a side-scrolling campaign.

overall_note: This is a strong rail overall: every candidate is a credible metroidvania comp, and the top end captures Solateria’s combat-forward, boss-centric exploration loop especially well.
anchor_summary: Solateria is a 2D action metroidvania built around interconnected exploration, ability-gated progression, boss-heavy combat, and especially timing-based parry/deflection play with build customization.

title: Collector's Cove

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Collector's Cove as a cozy, non-combat farming adventure centered on cultivating a floating farm, fishing, crafting, personalizing a ship-home, exploring islands, collecting entries for a compendium, and bonding with an animal companion. Multiple sources repeat the same core loop and explicitly state 'There are no enemies in this world,' which strongly supports a farming/life-sim identity over action or survival archetypes. The game appears single-player, casual, long-form, and exploration-driven, with progression through farm growth, collection, personalization, and companion relationship building.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's cozy farming/collecting audience reasonably well, with one especially strong oceanfaring match and several broader life-sim neighbors. The main gap is more games that combine farming with boat-based exploration and explicitly non-combat progression.

title: WWE 2K26

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify WWE 2K26 as a wrestling simulation/sports title centered on matches, roster-based competition, and multiple structured modes. Evidence repeatedly mentions new match types, PvP and co-op support, Showcase/MyRISE single-player campaigns, Universe/MyGM management layers, and MyFACTION team competition. Steam labels include Simulation and Sports, while the copy emphasizes winning matches, realistic wrestling presentation, and replayable seasonal/live-service-adjacent content. Although wrestling is not a dedicated archetype in the allowed list, sports_sim is the best fit for its core gameplay identity.

overall_note: The set is strongly aligned with the anchor’s gameplay identity. The recent WWE 2K entries are near-direct comps, and AEW: Fight Forever is a valid but looser wrestling neighbor due to its more arcade-first feel.
anchor_summary: WWE 2K26 is a modern licensed pro-wrestling sim built around ring matches, large rosters, simulation-leaning presentation, Showcase/MyRISE single-player content, Universe/MyGM sandbox management, creation tools, and online/local multiplayer.

title: 1348 Ex Voto

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a third-person cinematic action-adventure centered on a linear heroic rescue quest through semi-open historical-medieval Italy. Core play is melee sword combat with two combat stances, boss-oriented encounters, authored story progression, exploration of handcrafted locations, and light collection/progression via skill books and weapon customization. This strongly fits cinematic_action_adventure; character-action and soulslike traits are present but less central because the material emphasizes cinematic narrative journey and setpiece adventure over pure combo mastery or explicitly punishing soulslike structure.

overall_note: The rail mostly tracks the anchor's gameplay identity well: narrative-led third-person action-adventures with melee combat, progression, exploration, and notable bosses. The strongest comps are the modern cinematic action-adventure titles; weaker entries drift toward either more RPG/soulslike challenge structure or more corridor-setpiece action.

title: Solasta II

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Solasta II as a 'Turn-Based Tactical RPG' built around a faithful tabletop-style ruleset, with the player creating and guiding a party of 4 adventurers through a quest-driven campaign. The evidence strongly supports party control, tactical party-based combat, quest/exploration progression, and choice-driven CRPG structure. The text also indicates freer roaming across Neokos rather than purely linear mission flow, which fits a semi-open campaign CRPG better than a pure tactics game. Turn-based tactics is a strong secondary identity, but the party creation, roleplay framing, questing, and choice emphasis make party_based_crpg the better primary archetype.

overall_note: The rail is very strong. It includes the obvious predecessor and several close party-based CRPGs with turn-based tactical combat and campaign/choice structure. Ordering can be tightened slightly to prioritize the direct Solasta lineage and closest D&D-like party RPG fits.
anchor_summary: Solasta II is a party-based fantasy CRPG/tactical RPG built around a 4-character party, turn-based D20-style combat, quest-driven campaign progression, character building, exploration, and choice-led tabletop-inspired play.
gap_notes: Solasta: Crown of the Magister is an essential include and should lead the rail as the direct predecessor/base-game lineage comp.
Baldur's Gate 3 and Divinity: Original Sin 2 are especially strong because they combine party management, turn-based encounters, exploration, and quest/dialogue-driven CRPG structure.
Pathfinder: Wrath of the Righteous is a strong CRPG comparison, but its real-time-with-pause heritage and broader systems make it a little less directly aligned than the most turn-based-first comps.
Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader fits well on structure and turn-based party combat despite a very different setting; it is still meaningfully closer than a mere tone/franchise comp.

title: John Carpenter's Toxic Commando

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe a first-person 4-player co-op shooter focused on surviving and clearing massive undead/mutant hordes, reviving teammates, using classes/abilities, and traversing semi-open maps with drivable vehicles. The core loop reads much closer to cooperative horror-action than to military FPS, hero shooter, or arena FPS. Tone is pulpy rather than pure horror, but the infected/undead threat, survival framing, and squad-based horde combat strongly support co_op_horror as the closest taxonomy fit.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's clearest lane: cooperative undead horde shooters. World War Z and Back 4 Blood are the best fits, with World War Z especially close due to shared swarm-tech lineage and 4-player mission structure. The weaker point is that the set underrepresents the anchor's distinctive vehicle-driven semi-open-map layer, so some picks are solid genre neighbors rather than especially tight gameplay matches.
anchor_summary: John Carpenter's Toxic Commando is a 4-player co-op horde shooter built around first-person gunplay, class/ability roles, revives, objective-driven runs across semi-open maps, and heavy interaction with drivable vehicles while fighting massive undead swarms.
gap_notes: World War Z is the strongest comparator and should anchor the rail.
Back 4 Blood is a very strong fit on first-person 4-player campaign horde shooting, though its run/card structure differs from Toxic Commando's vehicle-forward identity.
Killing Floor 2 fits the co-op horde-FPS side but is more survival/wave-arena oriented than mission-and-traversal based.
Zombie Army 4 is a reasonable co-op undead-shooter comp, but its third-person sniper/action identity is looser than the top two.
None of the candidates strongly capture Toxic Commando's drivable-vehicle emphasis across semi-open maps, which remains a notable uncovered gameplay trait.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest benchmark: 4-player co-op, objective-based campaigns, class progression, and massive zombie swarms using the same Saber horde-tech lineage. Third-person instead of first-person, but overall loop is extremely close.

Very close on first-person 4-player co-op campaign structure, special infected pressure, team roles, and replayable horde shooting. Less aligned on vehicle use and more run/deck-driven in progression.
Strong class-based co-op FPS against dense monster hordes, but its core loop is wave survival/arena defense rather than squad traversal through semi-open mission spaces with vehicles.
Good undead co-op campaign shooter with progression and set-piece swarm fights, but the third-person sniper/action emphasis makes it a looser identity match than the more direct horde-FPS comps.

title: Piece By Piece

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store text consistently describes a cozy single-player shop-life loop: inherit and run a family repair shop, restore customer items, grow berries for paint, decorate the shop, bake cookies, clean and maintain the workspace, and interact with neighbors. The core identity is non-combat, low-pressure domestic/occupational simulation with customization and social-town flavor, which fits life_sim best. Repairing objects 'each with its own puzzle' adds a light hidden-object/puzzle-adjacent layer, while selling items and maintaining the store gives a management secondary aspect, but the evidence most strongly supports a cozy life-sim structure rather than a pure tycoon or puzzle game.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's restoration and cozy shopkeeping identity well, with two especially strong repair-focused matches. It is somewhat skewed toward pure restoration/crafting sims and lighter on games that also match the anchor's broader life-sim, domestic upkeep, and neighborly social loop.
anchor_summary: Cozy single-player repair-shop life sim centered on restoring customers' sentimental objects through light hands-on puzzles, with shop upkeep, customization, crafting/growing side tasks, and gentle town-social flavor.
gap_notes: Best candidates are those built around object restoration for clients in a relaxed solo structure.
Some picks match cozy artisan/shopkeeping but miss the anchor's specific repair-puzzle loop.
The rail could use more emphasis on low-pressure life-sim/shop-maintenance/social structure rather than broader exploration or sandbox crafting.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Directly matches sentimental object restoration, hands-on repair puzzles, cozy pacing, and helping townsfolk through their belongings.

Strong shopkeeping and artisan-crafting overlap with townsfolk customers and satisfying tool interaction, but potion brewing is a different core loop from object repair.
Shares cozy life-sim/shopkeeping, decorating, chores, and town-building elements, though it is much broader and more farming/exploration-driven than repair-focused.
Repairing friendly machines and low-pressure puzzles are relevant, but the core identity leans much more toward traversal-heavy adventure/exploration than shop-life restoration sim.

title: 1 CatLine

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored description clearly identifies the game as a 'brainteasing precision platformer' built around single-screen challenges across 50 unique levels. Core play is running and leaping through increasingly complex stages, with explicit platforming plus puzzle gating via freezing time, shifting perspectives, pushing crates, activating switches, and using the cat companion's body as an improvised platform. This strongly supports a precision_platformer classification with level-based, side-scrolling 2D, non-combat gameplay.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's precision-platforming side well, especially compact 2D challenge design, but it underrepresents the anchor's stronger puzzle-platforming identity and single-screen room logic. Best fits are games combining exacting movement with discrete room challenges; pure puzzle picks or more open exploratory platformers drift further away.
anchor_summary: 1 CatLine is a 2D level-based precision platformer built around single-screen challenge rooms, frequent retries, and puzzle-platforming verbs such as time freezing, perspective shifting, crate pushing, switch activation, and using a companion body as an improvised platform. It appears non-combat and heavily focused on compact execution-plus-brainteaser stages.
gap_notes: The rail leans heavily toward twitch precision platformers and is lighter on explicit puzzle-platformers with room-by-room object manipulation.
Single-screen stage structure is a key anchor trait; more sprawling or interconnected platformers are somewhat less precise fits.
The anchor appears non-combat and puzzle-gated, so candidates with stronger action/platforming emphasis fit less tightly than their genre proximity suggests.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on 2D room-based precision platforming, discrete challenge structure, and execution-heavy obstacle clearing. Less aligned on puzzle manipulation verbs, but the single-screen format matches especially well.
Excellent fit on level-based 2D precision platforming, short stages, high difficulty, and fast retries. More pure twitch platforming and less puzzle-centric than the anchor.

Strong overlap in challenging 2D traversal and puzzle-inflected room design, with a singular movement mechanic. The more interconnected exploration structure makes it less aligned than the best room-based level comp picks.
Shares hard 2D platforming, dense hazards, and repeated-death challenge progression. It is broader and more adventure-like than the anchor's tighter single-screen brainteaser structure.

Matches the single-screen puzzle structure and body-based spatial problem solving nicely, echoing the companion-platform idea. However, it is fundamentally a puzzle game rather than a run-and-jump precision platformer.

title: Fatal Frame II: Crimson Butterfly Remake

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe a Japanese-style horror adventure centered on exploring an abandoned haunted village, uncovering its secrets, and repelling ghosts with the Camera Obscura. The copy emphasizes horror atmosphere, darkness, exploration, escape, and updated combat elements rather than pure action, which strongly fits survival_horror. Single-player campaign structure is directly supported, and the village mystery plus ghost encounters suggest a guided exploratory survival-horror loop.

overall_note: The candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor’s gameplay identity. Two entries from the same camera-combat survival-horror lineage are obvious fits, and DreadOut is a strong external comp because it shares the unusual photo-based ghost-fighting loop and exploratory horror structure.
anchor_summary: Third-person Japanese survival-horror adventure focused on solitary exploration of a haunted village, puzzle/item progression, and defending against spirits with the Camera Obscura in a story-driven single-player campaign.
gap_notes: No direct base-game or expansion pairing is present, so the rail lacks a base_game benchmark.

title: Galactic Vault

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Galactic Vault as a single-player first-person shooter roguelite with fast-paced combat, permanent arsenal expansion, buildcraft via weapon attachments and upgrade combinations, boss encounters, and room-based infiltration through vaults. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not contain a clear FPS roguelite / boomer-shooter-roguelite bucket. The strongest candidate, arena_fps, is not well supported because the game is not described as match-based or arena-only and instead emphasizes single-player roguelite progression. Immersive_sim_action is also not sufficiently supported because the text does not establish systemic problem solving, multiple infiltration approaches, or immersive-sim interaction depth. Rejecting due to taxonomy fit gap rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: This candidate set matches the anchor's gameplay identity very well. The best fits are other first-person roguelite shooters with fast movement, room- or stage-based repeat runs, build synergies, and meta progression. Lower-ranked entries remain relevant, but drift somewhat through stronger hero/RPG framing or older boomer-shooter/dungeon-crawler variants.
anchor_summary: Galactic Vault is a single-player fast-paced FPS roguelite built around repeated run-based vault infiltration, high-mobility room combat, weapon/build customization through attachments and upgrades, boss encounters, and some permanent progression between attempts.
gap_notes: No exact base-game or DLC pairing is present.
Top candidates strongly cover the anchor's core loop: fast FPS combat, repeated runs, buildcraft, bosses, and persistent unlocks.

The weakest candidates still fit the FPS roguelite space, but some emphasize dungeon crawling, magic loadouts, or older arcade-boomer-shooter structure more than weapon-attachment buildcraft and tech-heist runs.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match: fast FPS roguelite runs, aggressive mobility, room-to-room combat, bosses, randomized stages, and persistent upgrades. Co-op is extra, but single-player identity aligns strongly.

title: Haunted Lands

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify Haunted Lands as a retro-inspired action platformer. The evidence strongly supports a side-scrolling, level-based single-player campaign built around platform traversal and dominant combat: players slay hordes of cultists and monsters, use six distinct characters with different close-range and ranged playstyles, acquire artifacts that alter abilities, uncover secrets, and fight challenging bosses. Horror setting and bleak/grotesque tone are explicit, but the core gameplay identity is much more action-platformer than horror-specialist archetypes. The prior computed candidate pointing to sports_sim is clearly spurious and contradicted by all textual evidence.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally correct: all candidates are retro-styled 2D action platformers with meaningful combat challenge. The best fits are games that match Haunted Lands on stage-based progression, multiple characters or varied combat styles, secrets, bosses, and punishing old-school structure. A couple of candidates lean more toward broader exploration or a more singular combat identity, making them slightly looser fits.
anchor_summary: Haunted Lands is a single-player retro-inspired 2D action platformer centered on side-scrolling stage progression, heavy combat against hordes and bosses, secrets, artifact-driven upgrades, multiple playable characters with different playstyles, and a high challenge level in a dark horror setting.
gap_notes: The strongest overlap for this anchor is classic side-scrolling action platformers with boss-heavy stage progression, secrets, and high difficulty.
Multiple playable characters or materially distinct combat styles are an important differentiator for the anchor and help Bloodstained: Curse of the Moon 2 stand out.
Infernax is close in tone and challenge but is looser structurally because its progression is more exploration/open-world driven than stage-based.
Valfaris and Slain: Back from Hell fit the brutal combat-platformer space well, but each is more centered on a fixed protagonist combat identity than on roster-based playstyle variety.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent gameplay match: retro 2D action platforming, stage-based progression, multiple playable characters, varied abilities, secrets, bosses, and a similarly old-school challenge model.
Very close on side-scrolling combat-platforming, grotesque enemies, upgrades, and intense boss encounters; less aligned on roster-based character variety but highly compatible in core loop.

Strong tonal and mechanical overlap in hardcore retro action platforming, secrets, upgrades, and bosses, but its more exploration-heavy/open-world structure is a meaningful step away from Haunted Lands' stage-driven identity.

A valid comp for punishing 2D action platforming with dark horror-fantasy stages and bosses, though it is a bit narrower and more methodical, with less emphasis on character variety and artifact-driven build shifts.

title: Ghost of Yōtei: Legends

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly establishes a cooperative multiplayer add-on with two-player story missions, four-player survival matches, class selection, map-based territory control, and wave-based enemy defense in a supernatural setting. However, the available text does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. It does not provide reliable evidence for perspective, traversal, long-term progression, or roguelite structure, and the DLC context does not include a parent game to inherit core gameplay identity from. The mode appears closer to a co-op wave/survival action experience, but that exact archetype is not available in the taxonomy, so assigning a nearest-fit archetype would be too speculative.

title: Kritter: Defend Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the core loop as a 'chaotic Roguelite Twin-Stick Shooter with a Base Defense Twist' playable with up to 4 players. The text describes repeated runs with time reversal, retaining experience/resources between attempts, which strongly supports roguelite run structure plus metaprogression. Moment-to-moment play centers on exploring a planet, gathering resources, fighting monsters in arena encounters and waves, then returning to defend the crashed ship using crafted ramparts, turrets, and traps. Combat is dominant, fast, and top-down/twin-stick in identity, with multiple class/loadout styles spanning melee, explosives, flamethrowers, turrets, missiles, dashes, and ranged attacks. Co-op is explicitly emphasized in Steam categories and main copy, making co_op_action_roguelite the best fit. The base-defense/crafting layer is meaningful but additive rather than enough to displace the action-roguelite identity.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor’s hybrid of co-op action and defense fairly well, with one especially close match and several solid structural neighbors. Biggest gaps are that some candidates drift toward survival ARPG, mission-based tower defense, or heavy base-building rather than the anchor’s top-down roguelite run loop.
anchor_summary: Co-op-first action roguelite built around top-down/twin-stick combat, timed exploration and resource gathering, then returning to defend a central ship with crafted turrets, traps, and fortifications under escalating waves, with run-based progression plus persistent meta-upgrades.
gap_notes: The best fits should emphasize top-down or similarly direct action combat plus explicit defend-a-core/base structure within repeatable runs.
Several candidates share defense/building themes but are looser on the anchor’s roguelite session structure and exploration-then-return cadence.
No exact same-game/base-game pairing is present; the rail leans on strong neighbors rather than an obvious must-include benchmark.

title: Mr. Sleepy Man

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mr. Sleepy Man as a 3D platformer with an expressive moveset ('jump, glide, and dive'), interconnected levels, heavy exploration, secrets, collectibles, and hundreds of objectives. The core loop centers on platform navigation, roaming semi-open handcrafted spaces, interacting with objects/NPCs, and completing collectathon-style objectives rather than combat. The tone is overtly comedic and whimsical, matching a 3D collectathon identity more strongly than a metroidvania. Action-platformer is a secondary fit because of the movement-focused traversal and chaos-oriented play, but collectible/exploration structure is the clearer primary gameplay identity.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: it centers on modern 3D platformer/collectathon comps and includes one smart adjacent sandbox-chaos pick. Best fits are movement-driven collectathons with exploration and dense objective hunting; the main gaps are that several candidates are more combat-structured than the anchor, and only Goat Simulator 3 really captures the reactive mischief/sandbox side.
anchor_summary: Third-person single-player 3D collectathon/platformer with a movement-focused moveset, semi-open/interconnected exploration, heavy objective and secret hunting, interactive NPC/object spaces, and a notable sandbox-chaos/mischief layer with relatively light combat emphasis.
gap_notes: The anchor's standout differentiator is chaos-simulator interaction with reactive NPCs; most candidates match collectathon traversal better than that systemic mischief layer.
Several candidates skew more combat- and boss-oriented than the anchor, which appears more platforming/exploration-first.
No candidate is a near-perfect match for the anchor's blend of 3D collectathon structure plus consequence-driven sandbox town disruption.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Premier 3D collectathon match: expressive movement, semi-open kingdoms, dense secrets, constant objective hunting, and interaction-led exploration strongly mirror the anchor's core loop.
Very close on 3D collectathon identity, platforming moveset, exploration, and collectible progression; somewhat more combat- and boss-driven than the anchor.
Strong fit on humorous third-person platforming, hub/world exploration, collectibles, and characterful spaces, but psychic combat/powers are more central than in the anchor.

title: Crabmeat

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crabmeat as a short first-person, mouse-only, point-and-click survival horror experience about operating a crabbing vessel, collecting quota within 7 days, navigating Antarctic waters, maintaining traps/ship functions, and defending against underwater threats. That is enough to infer a horror adventure with light survival/vehicle-management elements, but it does not cleanly map to any single allowed archetype. The current computed hidden_object_puzzle candidate appears overfit to point-and-click/puzzle signals and is not well supported by the actual described loop, while survival_horror/psychological_horror are also not securely established because the evidence does not clearly describe the genre-defining combat/resource/scare structure expected for those archetypes. Strong evidence exists for a hybrid first-person point-and-click horror adventure, but not for a confident placement into the available taxonomy archetypes.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of Crabmeat's nautical-horror and short-form experimental adventure identity, with DREDGE and Iron Lung as the clearest fits. The weaker spots are overvaluing pure point-and-click horror titles that miss the vessel-operation/work-loop side, and including one mystery adventure that shares shipboard exploration but not the anchor's survival-labor structure.
anchor_summary: Crabmeat is a short single-player first-person horror adventure built around mouse-only/point-and-click interaction, operating a small vessel, fulfilling a quota-driven work loop, light ship maintenance/navigation, environmental pressure, and puzzle/interface-led progression rather than combat-heavy action.
gap_notes: Best matches emphasize small-craft operation, hostile water navigation, and short-form dread more than generic point-and-click horror.
Several candidates share horror adventure structure but miss Crabmeat's quota/work loop and ship-management elements.
Return of the Obra Dinn is understandable on first-person shipboard mystery grounds, but it is much more deduction-centric than Crabmeat.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best overall fit: boat-based task loop, hazardous sea navigation, quest/progression structure, equipment upgrading, and looming maritime horror align well even though it is broader, more systemic, and less point-and-click.

Understandable via first-person shipboard exploration and mystery progression, but it is fundamentally a logical deduction game rather than a horror-tinged vessel-operation/work-loop adventure.

title: Granblue Fantasy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Granblue Fantasy as a classic JRPG with a deep turn-based combat system, elemental strategy, classes, party-building, character/weapon progression, and a main quest plus character story episodes. The text strongly supports a story-driven, party-command RPG identity over any other candidate. Real-time co-op raids are present, but they read as an additive mode layered onto the core authored campaign JRPG structure rather than replacing it. The fighting/match-based signals in the prior computation appear to be false positives from words like raids, combos, and co-op rather than evidence of a traditional fighter.

overall_note: The rail is directionally correct because both candidates match the anchor's turn-based, party-based JRPG identity better than action or fighter comps would. The main gap is that neither candidate reflects Granblue Fantasy's raid/co-op layer or broader class/build optimization particularly closely, so the set captures the campaign JRPG side more than the full product shape.
anchor_summary: Granblue Fantasy is best understood here as a story-driven JRPG with turn-based party combat, character/class and weapon progression, elemental strategy, and substantial authored content, with co-op raids as an important secondary mode rather than the core identity.
gap_notes: No candidate mirrors the anchor's notable online co-op raid component.
The set leans toward story-driven turn-based JRPGs but is lighter on class-switching and weapon-grid style progression parallels.
Given the limited candidate pool, both are defensible, but neither feels like an essential benchmark specifically for Granblue Fantasy's mixed solo-plus-raid structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very solid fit on strategic turn-based party combat, roster-building, ongoing story delivery, and character progression. It is somewhat looser because its structure emphasizes exploration and live-service progression more than Granblue's class/raid-driven JRPG loop.

title: Thysiastery

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player, turn-based, party-commanded dungeon-crawling roguelike RPG with permadeath, procedural generation, recruitable characters, and skill learning. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly fit this identity. The evidence does not establish key archetype-defining traits needed for turn_based_tactics or tactical_rpg, such as tactical-overhead presentation, explicit battlefield structure, or clearer tactical-RPG conventions; nor does it support jrpg_story_rpg, party_based_crpg, or card_battler. Because the gameplay is real but sits between supported archetypes and the strongest candidate mapping would be too inferential, classification should be rejected rather than forced.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's core identity reasonably well: turn-based dungeon pressure, party or roster management, and roguelike attrition. The best fits emphasize party-based expeditions and procedural/permadeath structure; weaker fits drift toward authored DRPGs or solo-focused roguelikes.
anchor_summary: Thysiastery is a single-player turn-based dungeon-crawling roguelike RPG centered on party management, recruiting characters, procedural labyrinth exploration, permadeath pressure, and deep skill/progression within repeated high-risk runs.
gap_notes: The rail leans well into party-based turn-based roguelike RPGs, which is the anchor's clearest identity.
A few candidates share dungeon crawling and turn-based combat but miss the anchor's procedural/permadeath run structure.
None of the candidates are outright wrong, but the lower half are more adjacent than truly same-identity comps.

title: Age of Wonders 4: Rise from Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted using DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC description is additive only: it adds a new culture, form, tomes, wildlife, terrain, quests, and realm traits, and does not describe a different core mode or genre. Parent-game evidence explicitly describes 'Age of Wonders’ signature blend of 4X strategy and turn-based tactical combat,' empire expansion over turns, realm exploration, customizable factions, city/empire growth, tactical battles, and replayable realm generation. The DLC copy reinforces the same identity with battlefield mobility, territory seizure, harvesting ancient wonders, new world-map content, epic quests, and new tomes. This strongly supports 4x_strategy as primary, with turn-based tactical battles as a secondary gameplay layer.

overall_note: The set contains the essential base game and several highly relevant neighboring fantasy 4X strategy titles with tactical combat. Ordering the top tier is straightforward, with only the lower-middle distinctions depending on how much weight to give full 4X structure versus campaign-led tactical strategy.
anchor_summary: Age of Wonders 4: Rise from Ruin is a DLC for a fantasy 4X strategy game built around turn-based empire expansion, realm exploration, faction customization, and separate tactical turn-based battles. Best matches should preserve that exact 4X-plus-tactical-combat identity rather than only sharing fantasy theme or turn-based pacing.
gap_notes: The rail correctly includes the parent game, which is the must-have comparison for a DLC anchor.
Series neighbors from Age of Wonders are especially strong because they share the same blend of empire management and tactical battles.
Lower-ranked candidates are still plausible, but some lean more toward campaign-led strategy/RPG structure than a full sandbox 4X loop.
