title: Fly for Fly

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single short Metacritic-style description describing a simple comedic action game where the player controls a fly, dodges swats, and annoys students in a classroom. While this suggests arcade-style evasive action with timing-heavy movement, the evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It does not clearly establish platforming, shooter structure, racing, horror, RPG systems, puzzle structure, tactics, sim management, or another supported core gameplay identity. Because the gameplay appears to fall outside the available archetype set and the source base is sparse, rejection is more reliable than forcing an imprecise fit.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's mischievous-chaos fantasy reasonably well, but only one candidate closely matches the anchor's insect-scale evade-and-annoy loop. The rest are progressively looser nuisance/sandbox comps that share tone and disruption more than the anchor's tight survival-action structure.
anchor_summary: Fly for Fly appears to be a small-scale comedic action game built around controlling a pest, buzzing around a confined environment, provoking human NPCs, and surviving reactive swats through timing-heavy evasive movement. Its core identity is immediate arcade-style nuisance play rather than broad sandbox destruction, stealth puzzling, or deep progression.
gap_notes: Only Mister Mosquito strongly matches the anchor's specific 'tiny flying pest dodging swats while harassing humans' gameplay identity.
Several candidates lean toward sandbox/objective puzzling or physics destruction rather than immediate arcade survival and irritation-meter pressure.
The rail is usable, but it is somewhat over-indexed on comedic nuisance tone instead of the anchor's more precise evasive-action loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct gameplay match: you play as a small annoying insect targeting humans while avoiding swats and household countermeasures in compact spaces.

title: Packing Life

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports a cozy single-player non-combat puzzle/simulation game centered on organizing, rotating, and fitting items into boxes under space constraints, with light story and workspace customization. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The computed near-match of hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the text, which describes spatial packing/optimization rather than hidden object search, point-and-click investigation, or mystery solving. Because the available evidence is strong but indicates an out-of-taxonomy organization/packing puzzle rather than an existing archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate fit.

overall_note: The rail has a very strong top comp and several solid organization-puzzle neighbors, but it gets looser after the first few entries because some candidates shift toward broader tidying, warehouse retrieval, or inventory-management logic rather than the anchor's cozy box-packing identity.
anchor_summary: Packing Life is a cozy single-player non-combat organization puzzle/sim about rotating and fitting varied items into constrained boxes, with light progression, story, and customization. Its closest comps should emphasize spatial packing/placement, tidy optimization, and relaxing structure over combat, action pressure, or unrelated puzzle forms.
gap_notes: Unpacking is the clearest same-audience benchmark and should anchor the rail.
A Little to the Left fits the organizing/tidying appeal well, but it is more observational arrangement than constrained packing optimization.
Wilmot's Warehouse shares warehouse organization and sorting, though its memory/retrieval loop and systemic pressure make it less cozy and less box-packing-specific.
inbento is a good spatial-fitting neighbor, but its recipe-reconstruction structure is more abstract and pattern-driven than packing simulation.
Save Room has strong shape-fitting overlap, but the survival-inventory optimization and resource logic pull it away from the anchor's relaxed cozy identity.

title: Peregrino

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Peregrino as an isometric survival management game centered on crossing a cursed land, scavenging, hunting, fighting, managing scarce resources, inventory pressure, nightfall danger, and extracting with loot. The horror identity is strong via grotesque abominations, cursed woods, and survival-focused threat escalation after dusk. The game also has clear party/caravan management elements: caring for companions, leading pilgrims, making fate-affecting decisions, and upgrading caravan crafting stations. That makes it broader than pure survival horror, so survival_horror fits best as the primary archetype with tactical_rpg and party_based_crpg as secondary influences rather than full identities.

overall_note: The set captures Peregrino's two strongest poles reasonably well: top-down survival horror pressure and caravan/party survival management. Best candidates match either the day/night survival loop or the traveling-party attrition structure; weaker ones lean more toward tactical RPG or mercenary sandbox play than horror-survival management.
anchor_summary: Peregrino appears to be an isometric single-player survival-management game built around day exploration and night defense, scarce resource scavenging, caravan/camp upgrades, companion care, harsh decision-making, and horror pressure from escalating monsters. Its identity is broader than pure survival horror because party/caravan management and expedition planning are central to the loop.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest on survival-management and caravan leadership, but only one candidate closely combines both horror pressure and expedition logistics.
Several candidates skew toward tactical RPG/CRPG structure, which overlaps with party travel and attrition but misses Peregrino's scavenging, extraction, and nightfall survival cadence.
No candidate here is a near-identical match for Peregrino's blend of isometric horror, caravan upkeep, companion care, and loot-extraction survival loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent match on top-down survival horror, daytime scavenging, nighttime shelter defense, crafting, scarcity, and escalating darkness pressure. Lacks Peregrino's caravan/party management focus.

Very strong on survivor management, shelter upkeep, day/night structure, scavenging, crafting, and harsh survival decisions. Less aligned on direct combat feel, horror framing, and overland caravan expedition structure.

Strong caravan leadership, route planning, crew welfare, hostile-world travel, and survival attrition make it a sensible comp. More of a narrative trading/CRPG sandbox than a horror-driven scavenging survival game.

Good overlap in caravan journey, resource pressure, consequential decisions, and managing followers on a harsh trek. Core identity is still narrative tactical RPG with set-piece battles, not systemic survival-management horror.

Party travel, camp upkeep, crafting, attrition, and survival in a hostile world are relevant. But its core loop is open-world mercenary tactics and contract play, which is notably farther from Peregrino's horror-survival caravan journey.

title: Pieced Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a short, single-player scrapbook puzzler built around sorting mementos, reassembling torn photos, and solving gentle logic/word puzzles across handcrafted pages while uncovering an authored story about friendship and aging. There is no combat, the tone is explicitly cozy and emotional, and interaction appears cursor-driven through scrapbook pages. While the copy does not strongly emphasize classic hidden-object searching, the closest supported taxonomy fit is hidden_object_puzzle because the core loop is light object-focused puzzle interaction in handcrafted scenes/pages with narrative progression.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: it captures cozy, object-centric, low-pressure puzzle games with light narrative framing. Unpacking is the clearest same-audience comp, and several others work as neighbors. The main weakness is that some candidates drift from intimate storybook/object-handling play into broader organization or interactive-fiction territory.
anchor_summary: Pieced Together is a short, cozy, single-player narrative puzzler built around handling personal objects inside scrapbook pages: sorting mementos, reassembling photos, solving gentle logic/word puzzles, and lightly decorating while an authored memoir-like story unfolds. Its identity is low-pressure tactile puzzle interaction with sentimental objects, not challenge-heavy logic, combat, or open-ended creativity.
gap_notes: Unpacking is the strongest benchmark here and should anchor the rail.
A Little to the Left and Assemble With Care are strong neighboring fits because they share tactile object-focused puzzle play, though each misses part of the anchor's memoir-scrapbook identity.
Wilmot's Warehouse shares sorting/organization but differs more on pressure, abstraction, and progression structure.
Behind the Frame overlaps on short, cozy, emotional authored pacing, but its play pattern is more interactive fiction/art puzzling than object-sorting scrapbook puzzles.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close fit on cozy single-player object manipulation, gentle puzzle gating, environmental arrangement, and intimate story revealed through possessions.
Very strong match on tactile sorting/arrangement puzzles and low-pressure casual play, though it is much less narrative-driven and less autobiographical in structure.
Short, cozy, story-led puzzle game centered on meaningful objects; repair/disassembly differs from scrapbook sorting, but the authored emotional progression is very compatible.

Shares organization-first puzzle play and object sorting, but it is more systemic, abstract, and efficiency-oriented, with upgrades and optional co-op pulling it away from the anchor's gentle memoir format.

title: Mirage 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Mirage 7 as a third-person, single-player story adventure centered on Nadira’s journey through a desert setting, with authored narration, environmental puzzle solving, collectible-item crafting, and melee/ranged combat against monsters. That combination supports a cinematic action-adventure identity more strongly than RPG or open-world labels. World topology is not stated explicitly, but the copy emphasizes a guided tale and handcrafted challenges rather than sandbox or systemic play.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of Mirage 7's story-driven third-person action-adventure identity. Kena is the clearest match, and the rest are mostly reasonable neighbors, though the set leans a bit broad once it moves into puzzle-adventure and stealth-heavy territory.
anchor_summary: Mirage 7 reads as a single-player third-person cinematic action-adventure: authored story progression, environmental puzzle solving, light crafting/tool use, traversal through a guided world, and meaningful melee/ranged combat against monsters. Best comps should preserve that story-led adventure structure rather than drift into pure puzzle adventure, stealth-survival, or heavily systemic shooter/action RPG territory.
gap_notes: There is no true must-keep benchmark here; the list lacks an especially obvious near-identical comp anchored on story-driven third-person action-adventure with puzzles and moderate combat.
RiME is a good tonal and structural comparison for guided exploration and mythic journey, but its lack of combat makes it a looser gameplay match than the rail positioning suggests.
A Plague Tale: Innocence overlaps on linear narrative adventure and crafted problem-solving, but its stealth-survival loop is notably different from Mirage 7's direct melee/ranged combat emphasis.
ReCore shares third-person action-adventure and companion/puzzle gating, but it is more combat- and systems-forward than Mirage 7's cinematic adventure pitch.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest fit on third-person story-led action-adventure, guided exploration, environmental puzzles, collectibles/upgrades, and mixed melee/ranged combat; more combat-forward, but audience overlap is strong.

Strong match on cinematic single-player adventure structure, traversal, environmental hazards, light crafting/upgrades, and direct combat, though Tomb Raider is much more set-piece-heavy and shooter-oriented.

Shares third-person action-adventure, desert/sci-fi exploration, companion-enabled progression, and combat plus traversal gating, but it is more systemic, combat-centric, and shooter-leaning than Mirage 7.

Reasonable overlap on linear narrative adventure, young protagonist journey, crafted tools, and environmental problem-solving, but the core loop is stealth-survival and avoidance far more than action-adventure combat.
Good match for guided mythic journey, environmental puzzles, and emotional exploration, but the missing combat layer makes it a noticeably weaker gameplay comp for Mirage 7.

title: Fortuna Magus

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence consistently describes Fortuna Magus as a fantasy JRPG with a rich authored story, single-player campaign structure, and strategic turn-based party battles. Core gameplay details are concrete and repeated across Steam short/detailed descriptions and Metacritic: characters pursue a missing-father narrative, battles revolve around Revelations, TP management, tandem attacks, and learning new spells/skills through combat conditions and element thresholds. That supports a story-led JRPG identity with party management, magic-focused turn-based combat, quest-driven progression, and skill customization. Alternative candidates like turn_based_tactics are weaker because there is no evidence of grid tactics or overhead tactical structure, while monster_collect_rpg lacks creature-collection evidence.

overall_note: The set stays mostly on-target for story-driven turn-based JRPGs. The best fits are other compact, linear party-based JRPGs with strong progression systems, especially Kemco-style titles. A few candidates are solid but broader retro-JRPG neighbors rather than especially tight gameplay matches.
anchor_summary: Fortuna Magus is a single-player, story-led turn-based JRPG built around party management, authored progression, skill customization, and battle-specific resource/condition systems like TP, tandem attacks, elemental growth, and unlockable abilities.
gap_notes: The rail would be stronger with more direct Kemco/EXE-CREATE-style turn-based JRPG comps centered on compact campaign structure and layered skill unlock systems.
Top candidates fit the anchor's JRPG identity well, but not all reflect the anchor's specific combat-condition learning and elemental progression hooks.
No obvious bad mismatch is present, but some picks are more general retro-JRPG comparisons than especially close structural matches.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: traditional fantasy turn-based JRPG, fixed party progression, linear story campaign, and Kemco-style character/skill system emphasis align strongly with Fortuna Magus.

title: Messy Up

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes a cute multiplayer pet-chaos game with co-op, PvP, and asymmetric roles (pets cause destruction, owner catches/defends), plus collectibles and a home-building mode. However, the material does not clearly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The strongest signals are party/co-op/social chaos, but the evidence does not concretely define a match-based party-game structure, nor does it support action-adventure, platformer, shooter, racer, sim, or RPG archetypes in the allowed list. The current taxonomy audit already flagged a provider gap with no candidate archetypes. Because the gameplay description is broad, mode-mixed, and not strongly aligned to a supported archetype, classification should be rejected for now.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's animal-mischief and social-chaos angle fairly well, but most candidates miss the anchor's specific blend of household destruction, casual party controls, and asymmetric pet-vs-owner play. Best fits are sandbox destruction or indoor pet-mischief games; combat-centric or shooter-centric picks are notably weaker.
anchor_summary: Messy Up is a casual multiplayer animal-chaos game centered on pets wrecking household spaces, with co-op pet play and an asymmetric owner-vs-pets defense/chase mode. Its identity is environmental mischief and destruction with light abilities, low input complexity, and party-friendly social play rather than combat-heavy brawling or shooter action.
gap_notes: The rail lacks a closer asymmetric household sabotage/defense comparison; none mirror the pet-vs-owner loop particularly well.
Several candidates match tone or animal chaos, but diverge on core structure: open-world sandbox, stealth puzzle, brawler, or shooter round play.

Good match on playful animal mischief, human obstruction, and sandbox-like prank interactions, with some co-op support. Less direct because its core loop is task-based stealth/puzzle prank execution, not destruction-heavy party chaos.

Shares accessible multiplayer chaos, cute animals, physics, and party-session appeal. But its gameplay identity is a combat-focused brawler/party game, not environmental wrecking or asymmetric chase/defense.

title: Banquet for Fools

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Banquet for Fools as a single-player, open-world, party-based RPG centered on investigating a village disappearance across the island of Invimona. Core loop evidence is strong: exploring towns and holds for clues, taking quests/requests, managing four companions with stat-driven dialogue options and bonding, and progressing characters through skills, spells, songs, equipment, and level-ups. Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally solid because all four candidates are party-oriented RPGs with exploration and progression overlap, but it leans too heavily toward broad party-RPG comps and underserves the anchor's specific mix of mystery-led island investigation and brawl-flavored combat.
anchor_summary: Banquet for Fools appears to be a single-player, party-based action RPG built around open-world island exploration, mystery-solving, companion management, and brawl-style combat with pause-assisted tactical input and leader switching. Its identity sits closest to western narrative party RPGs with a notable beat-'em-up/action layer, rather than a classic CRPG or JRPG.
gap_notes: The set captures party-based narrative RPG structure well, but only partially reflects the anchor's beat-'em-up combat identity.

title: Cupiclaw

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

overall_note: The proposed rail has several credible neighbors for Cupiclaw’s run-based combo-building arcade identity, with Ballionaire and Luck be a Landlord standing out most. The main weakness is drift toward broader roguelike/combo games where combat or card-play is the true core loop rather than machine-management and arcade-system manipulation.
anchor_summary: Cupiclaw is a single-player arcade/strategy roguelike built around claw-machine runs: grabbing prizes, drafting/removing rewards, upgrading the claw, and creating escalating combo engines across increasingly chaotic machines. Its closest fits should emphasize run-based machine interaction, physics/arcade systems, and synergy-driven buildcraft more than combat, theme, or generic roguelite structure.
gap_notes: Best fits here are other run-based machine or physics systems with strong synergy drafting; Ballionaire is the clearest match.
Balatro is strong on combo economics and run structure, but poker-hand deckbuilding is a more abstract scoring loop than Cupiclaw’s tactile claw-machine play.
Peglin and Backpack Hero both lean more heavily on combat than the anchor, which makes them looser comps despite shared roguelite progression and buildcraft.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: physics-driven arcade runs, drafting pieces into a machine-like board, and breaking scoring through chaotic synergies closely mirror Cupiclaw’s tactile combo-engine identity.
Excellent match on roguelite run structure, additive drafting, and machine-based payoff combos. Less tactile and physics-forward than Cupiclaw, but the slot-machine buildcraft is highly compatible.
Strong on escalating score engines, random-system exploitation, and run-based synergy construction, but its core play is poker-hand card optimization rather than arcade-machine manipulation.
Shares physics aiming, relic/orb synergies, and roguelite progression, yet its central loop is turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw’s machine-focused combo play.
Has run-based build shaping and spatial optimization, but inventory tactics and dungeon combat define the experience more than arcade-machine interaction or physics-driven prize play.

title: Bulb Boy 2: Jar of Despair

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

overall_note: The candidate set is well aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity as a horror-tinged point-and-click puzzle adventure, and it correctly includes the obvious must-have predecessor. Best fits preserve the same cursor-driven puzzle loop and linear story progression; weaker fits drift toward episodic comedy or more branching/replay-oriented structures.
anchor_summary: Bulb Boy 2: Jar of Despair is a single-player 2D point-and-click horror adventure centered on cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, linear authored progression, and grotesque cartoon presentation, with horror serving a puzzle-led adventure structure rather than combat-first play.
gap_notes: Bulb Boy is the clear must-include benchmark as the direct predecessor with near-identical gameplay identity.
The set is strong on horror point-and-click adventures, though some candidates lean more psychological/narrative-heavy or more nonlinear than the anchor.
The Darkside Detective is the loosest fit because its bite-size comedic case structure differs meaningfully from the anchor's sustained linear horror progression.

title: Ratcheteer DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently describe an interconnected action-adventure with more than 250 rooms across multiple regions and dungeons, eight tools that unlock new paths, and explicit traversal verbs like gliding, dashing, drilling, and stomping. The structure of gated exploration through a connected world plus boss encounters strongly supports metroidvania. The copy also supports a 2D platform-navigation-forward presentation and single-player campaign progression.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's core identity reasonably well: mostly single-player exploration-driven action adventures with gating, bosses, and progression through a connected world. The strongest fits are side-view, tool-driven platform-exploration games; the weaker fits skew more top-down Zelda-like or combat/discovery-led than the anchor.
anchor_summary: Ratcheteer DX is a single-player 2D action-adventure/metroidvania built around interconnected room-to-room exploration, platform traversal, tool-based ability gating, dungeon progression, and boss fights, with a lo-fi retro presentation.
gap_notes: The rail would be stronger with more side-view 2D metroidvania/action-platformer comps rather than top-down Zelda-like adventures.
Several candidates share item-gated exploration and dungeons, but fewer match the anchor's specific platform-forward traversal verbs and room-by-room side-scrolling structure.
No candidate is a near-identical 'same' fit; most are strong or adjacent neighbors rather than obvious benchmark twins.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on side-view action-platforming, wrench/tool identity, gated routes, big-world progression, and boss-heavy campaign flow.

Strong room-to-room exploration, dungeons, light combat, and item-gated progression, but it leans more top-down Zelda-like than platform-forward metroidvania.
Interconnected world, bosses, secrets, and item progression fit, but its isometric view, heavier combat mastery, and denser puzzle-discovery layer make it looser.

title: Docked

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Docked as a realistic port-operations simulator centered on operating heavy machinery, completing mission-based jobs and contracts, repairing equipment, expanding infrastructure, purchasing lots and machinery, and growing a logistics business. That supports transport_sim most strongly, with a management layer as secondary rather than primary. The current computed taxonomy over-indexes on racing because of vehicle language, but the actual gameplay loop is commercial cargo handling and operational expansion, not racing competition.

overall_note: The rail captures Docked's heavy-machinery, contract-driven simulation identity fairly well. Best fits emphasize hands-on equipment operation, logistics tasks, and business/infrastructure growth. Weaker picks lean too far toward off-road traversal challenge or VR-specific vehicle simulation rather than port operations and company-building.

gap_notes: The strongest candidates are work-sim and logistics-sim titles with contract progression and fleet/business growth.

title: The Walking Trade

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present the game as a shop/store management sandbox first: designing the shop layout, organizing shelves, setting prices, managing staff, crafting stock and upgrades, and building barricades/traps. That strongly fits management_tycoon with systemic sandbox/base growth. The zombie defense layer is significant but appears additive to the management loop rather than the main identity; combat is described as defending the shop during raids and hordes, not as the core session structure. Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's two main poles—shopkeeping and zombie survival—but the strongest fits are the games centered on running a store. The zombie-survival picks share setting and defense pressures, yet miss the anchor's retail optimization loop, so they fit better as looser neighbors than top recommendations.
anchor_summary: The Walking Trade is best understood as a shop-management/survival hybrid: designing and optimizing a retail space, stocking and pricing goods, managing workers, crafting upgrades, and defending the shop during zombie raids. Strong comps should preserve the business-management core first, with combat/base defense as a meaningful but secondary layer.
gap_notes: The best-fit candidates are the shop sims, because shelf layout, stocking, pricing, and customer flow are the anchor's clearest core loop.
Several candidates over-index on zombie survival/community management; those share theme and some systems, but not the anchor's merchant-first identity.
The rail has no truly near-identical benchmark combining hands-on shop sim with active base defense, so top picks are split between strong shop comps and looser survival hybrids.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very strong match on shop layout, stocking, pricing, crafting, expansion, and customer-service loops. It lacks the zombie defense/combat layer, but it best matches the anchor's merchant-management identity.

Shares survivor recruitment, resource gathering, base development, and zombie defense, which map to the anchor's survival side. However, it lacks the core retail/shop-optimization loop that defines the anchor.

title: Sushi Cat - Tower Defense

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies the game as a roguelite tower defense: placing towers, walls, and traps, shaping enemy paths, surviving wave-based encounters, drafting Boon Cards, fighting boss enemies each run, and unlocking permanent upgrades between runs. That is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 does not include a tower defense / tower-defense roguelite archetype. Available archetypes such as rts, turn_based_tactics, card_battler, or co_op_action_roguelite would misrepresent the core play loop. Because the evidence is strong yet does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The candidate is a very close gameplay match because it also centers on roguelite tower defense runs with path-shaping placement decisions, wave defense, randomized run structure, and replayability through unlocks and modifiers.
anchor_summary: Sushi Cat - Tower Defense is a single-player roguelite tower defense game built around short runs, wave defense on a single evolving map, maze-building with walls/traps/towers, boss waves, run-defining boon/card drafting, and meta-progression unlocks between runs.
gap_notes: The anchor emphasizes a single evolving level, boss encounters, and explicit meta-progression/talent upgrades, while the candidate leans more into tile-placement/board-game puzzle structure.
The candidate is slightly more puzzle-forward in its tile-draw and expanding-island format than the anchor's more direct tower/wall/trap maze-building loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Roguelite tower defense with short replayable runs, randomized build pieces, path-building, wave defense, and unlock-driven progression; one of the clearest genre matches available.

title: Never Grave: The Witch and the Curse

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'Metroidvania x Roguelite' with side-scrolling 2D action, exploration emphasis, traversal unlocks like double-jump, dungeon obstacles and puzzles, and intense boss fights. The core loop also includes procedurally generated dungeon runs, loot/material collection, and village rebuilding with facilities, crops, cooking, and skill-tree growth, which supports roguelite metaprogression as a secondary identity rather than displacing the metroidvania core. Up to 4-player co-op is explicit, but the strongest gameplay identity remains exploration-driven action-platforming with ability-gated progression.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor’s metroidvania-plus-roguelite identity fairly well, with especially good matches around procedural 2D exploration-combat. title: Pokémon Pokopia

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single promotional description emphasizing a cozy rebuilding premise: playing as Ditto, crafting, learning Pokémon moves, and restoring a desolate world. While this suggests buildcraft and systemic world-shaping, it does not concretely establish the core gameplay loop, perspective, session structure, management depth, or whether the title plays primarily as life sim, farming sim, city/colony builder, or another simulation form. With only sparse, high-level copy and no stronger gameplay-specific evidence, classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center of gravity around crafting and restoration games, with Dragon Quest Builders 2 as the clearest fit. It gets weaker as it shifts toward broader cozy life-sims that share tone and customization more than the likely build-and-rebuild gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Pokémon Pokopia appears to be a cozy third-person rebuilding/crafting game centered on gathering resources, using Pokémon abilities to reshape terrain, and restoring a desolate world into a livable community. The strongest comparisons should emphasize hands-on building, crafting-driven progression, exploration, and directed settlement/world restoration over pure social life-sim or passive decoration.
gap_notes: The set is strongest on cozy restoration and crafting, but only the Dragon Quest Builders games clearly match character-scale building as a primary loop.
Several candidates lean more toward life-sim/social routines than terrain shaping or construction-led progression.
No candidate here cleanly confirms whether Pokopia is more workshop sim, block-builder, or life-sim, so the best fits are still somewhat inferential.

Strong overlap in collecting materials, crafting infrastructure, completing town projects, and reviving a damaged community, but its workshop sim, relationship systems, and stronger combat focus make it looser.

title: Oceanhorn 3: Legend of the Shadow Sea

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only a broad open-world action/adventure premise: sailing across islands, battling giant Titans, and pursuing a long-lost god. However, the DB evidence is too sparse to confidently place it into a specific V2 archetype. Core identity axes needed for acceptance remain ungrounded, especially perspective, concrete combat style/tempo, progression model, and whether the RPG framing is substantial enough for open_world_fantasy_action_rpg versus broader open_world_action_adventure. With only a short Metacritic description and one normalized genre label, the classification would be too inferential.

overall_note: The rail has solid thematic and gameplay-adjacent picks, with the two prior Oceanhorn games as the most natural inclusions. The broader open-world comps also make sense, though they are looser fits because they emphasize larger systemic open-world play rather than the series' more curated island-adventure structure.
anchor_summary: Oceanhorn 3: Legend of the Shadow Sea appears to be a single-player fantasy action-adventure/action-RPG centered on sailing between islands, open-world or open-zone exploration, real-time combat against large creatures, puzzle/quest progression, and traversal features like gliding. Evidence is sparse, but the clearest identity is Zelda-like exploratory action-adventure rather than a pure story RPG.
gap_notes: The best fits are the franchise neighbors; they should anchor the rail.
Breath of the Wild and Immortals Fenyx Rising are useful exploratory-action comps, but both are broader and more systems-driven than the likely Oceanhorn 3 loop.
No candidate looks clearly wrong, but the set leans a bit too hard on open-world fantasy adjacency versus tighter Zelda-like action-adventure structure.

title: Avenue Escape

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Avenue Escape as a level-based traffic-management puzzle game: 50 stages, directing cars through intersections, timing moves, avoiding accidents, and optimizing clears. That supports a puzzle/logic identity, but the available taxonomy archetype list does not include a general traffic or route-planning puzzle category. The game does not strongly fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, pinball, rhythm_game, transport_sim, or other listed archetypes without forcing a mismatch.

overall_note: The candidate set captures the anchor's traffic-flow puzzle identity reasonably well, with one especially strong direct match and two broader transport/logistics neighbors. anchor_summary: Avenue Escape is a level-based traffic-management puzzle game focused on sequencing vehicle movement through intersections, timing actions, avoiding crashes, and clearing handcrafted stages efficiently.
gap_notes: Urban Flow is the clearest same-lane comp because it also centers on direct traffic-light/intersection control in discrete levels.
Mini Motorways overlaps on traffic optimization but shifts toward road-network design and run-based city growth rather than fixed puzzle stages.

title: eBaseball: PRO SPIRIT

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions and labels consistently identify this as a realistic baseball simulation. Official copy repeatedly emphasizes 'real baseball,' lifelike stadium/player presentation, exhibition play, online matches, rankings, and team-building via TEAM EDIT with roster selection and tournament regulations. Source labels include 'Baseball Sim' and baseball theme, while Steam categories support single-player, PvP, co-op, and split-screen/social play. The gameplay loop is match-based competition with realistic sports simulation rather than party-game framing.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally correct: all three candidates are baseball games and at least two are very close gameplay fits. The main gap is that the rail mixes true sim peers with a more arcade-leaning baseball title, so the set is solid but not perfectly tight around the anchor's realism-first identity.
anchor_summary: eBaseball: PRO SPIRIT is a realistic baseball simulation built around full baseball matches, exhibition play, online head-to-head competition, and team/roster construction under rulesets. The best comps should therefore be other baseball games with a similar sim-first match loop, presentation, multiplayer profile, and progression structure.
gap_notes: MLB The Show 24 and MLB The Show 25 are the clearest fits because they share licensed, realism-oriented baseball simulation, exhibition/head-to-head play, and deeper team/player progression structures.
Super Mega Baseball 4 is a valid baseball neighbor, but its arcade-leaning handling, stylization, and tone make it looser than the MLB The Show pair for a realism-first anchor.

title: Homura Hime

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy consistently describes an anime-style 3D action game centered on combo attacks, dodging/parrying, mixed close-range and ranged offense, and surviving bullet-hell projectile patterns against powerful demon-girl bosses. The emphasis on fast combo combat and boss-focused encounters strongly fits character_action. Campaign/story framing, single-player mode, weapon/equipment unlocks, and authored narrative further support this reading. No evidence points more strongly to soulslike, shooter, horror, or RPG-first structures.

overall_note: The rail has a solid character-action core and includes two especially strong fits, but it underweights the anchor's explicit bullet-hell boss-pattern identity. The best candidates are those combining stylish 3D action with heavy projectile-dodging pressure rather than just general anime action-RPG structure.

gap_notes: Bayonetta and Furi are the clearest fits because they best match fast 3D action plus dodge/parry-centric projectile pressure.

title: Rotwood

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Rotwood as a 1-4 player hack-and-slash roguelike/dungeon crawler built around repeat runs, co-op combat, boss fights, weapon mastery, gear upgrading, and bringing spoils back to craft equipment and improve a safe haven. The text explicitly says 'every run offers different powers and abilities,' calls it a 'rogue-like dungeon crawler,' emphasizes combat mastery over randomness, and highlights increasingly difficult encounters and powerful corrupted bosses. Metacritic also labels it a '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' which supports the side-scrolling/beat-em-up secondary read, but the stronger identity is cooperative action roguelite due to run-based structure, repeatable progression, and meta/base advancement.

overall_note: The set captures Rotwood’s co-op action-roguelite side fairly well, with two especially close matches and a couple of looser action-roguelite neighbors. The main gap is that only some candidates also reflect Rotwood’s side-on beat-'em-up/brawler identity and boss-centric combat mastery focus.
anchor_summary: Rotwood is a 1-4 player co-op action roguelite/hack-and-slash with a strong 2D brawler streak: run-based encounters across discrete locations, weapon mastery, gear/build progression, escalating challenge, and boss-centric combat that emphasizes skill execution over pure randomness.

title: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DLC descriptions consistently describe a combat-centric action game built around three new campaign chapters, unique levels, new weapons, bosses, fiend enemies, deadly trials, and an endurance-style challenge mode with successive combat encounters. The weapon copy emphasizes ranged attack, crowd control, rapid strikes, closing distance, and testing skilled 'master ninja' play, which strongly supports a fast, mastery-heavy character-action identity. The campaign/chapter structure and boss/trial language support linear level-based progression rather than open-world or RPG-first play. Horror imagery is present in the setting and story framing, but the gameplay evidence is much more strongly centered on stylish high-intensity combat than on horror as the primary play identity.

overall_note: The rail has the essential inclusion in the base game and two credible character-action neighbors. It is directionally correct, but beyond the base game the set leans more toward broader stylish-action comps than near-identical Ninja Gaiden-style fits.
anchor_summary: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters is a direct DLC extension of a fast, mission/chapter-based character-action game: high-speed melee combat, weapon-specific movesets, boss encounters, trials, and an endurance challenge mode built around precision and mastery.
gap_notes: The base game is an essential inclusion and clearly the best match.
The remaining candidates are good character-action comparisons, but they are genre neighbors rather than especially tight DLC-to-gameplay matches.
The set is small and becomes noticeably looser after the base game.

Another premier stylish action match with fast dodge-combo combat, set-piece battles, and score/challenge-driven replay, though its combat feel is a bit more spectacle-forward than Ninja Gaiden’s harsher precision.

title: Apopia: Sugar Coated Tale

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player hand-drawn narrative adventure with puzzles, rhythm segments, minigames, light action, and character-focused mystery. However, the copy does not establish a stable core gameplay loop, perspective, traversal model, or dominant mechanics strongly enough to support any allowed V2 archetype. It is not clearly a visual novel, hidden-object puzzle game, action-platformer, psychological horror title, or cinematic action-adventure based on the available text. The gameplay appears intentionally mixed and provider copy stays broad, so the evidence is insufficient for a confident archetype assignment.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's story-driven whimsical-dark indie adventure space reasonably well, with the best candidates emphasizing character interaction, playful mechanics, and emotional mystery. The main gap is that several picks lean too hard toward platforming or RPG combat compared with Apopia's broader adventure/minigame mix.
anchor_summary: Single-player hand-drawn narrative adventure with a mixed structure: exploration, puzzle solving, rhythm/minigame sequences, light action, and strong character-driven mystery. The clearest identity is whimsical-but-dark story adventure rather than a stable combat-heavy or platform-heavy genre fit.
gap_notes: The set trends toward narrative/tone matches more than exact gameplay-loop matches.
A stronger rail would prioritize adventure games with puzzle/minigame-led progression over RPG combat or traversal-heavy platformers.

title: Scott Pilgrim EX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Classification is strongly supported by the stored store/editorial copy plus a direct Metacritic genre label of '2D Beat-'Em-Up.' The text repeatedly frames the game as a 'brand-new brawling adventure' centered on fighting through Toronto, stringing together stylish combos, using weapons and special attacks, battling enemies and bosses, and supporting up to four-player co-op. It also specifies a 2D beat-'em-up identity, seven fighters with distinct combat styles, and a city of interconnected levels with quests and hidden areas, which fits a campaign-driven beat_em_up with semi-open progression and light action-adventure structure rather than a pure linear stage arcade brawler. Progression is reinforced by badges, stat upgrades, coins, and customizable builds.

overall_note: The rail is solid and correctly focused on modern beat-'em-ups. Its strongest hit is River City Girls 2 because it mirrors not just the brawling but also the connected-world, quest, shop, and progression structure. The rest are valid genre neighbors, though TMNT and Streets of Rage 4 skew more arcade-linear than the anchor, and Castle Crashers is a looser but still understandable co-op brawler comp.
anchor_summary: Scott Pilgrim EX is a modern 2D beat-'em-up centered on combo-driven melee combat, multiple distinct fighters, light build/stat progression, quests, interconnected city exploration, and up-to-4-player drop-in co-op. Best matches should preserve the side-scrolling brawler core loop first, with extra credit for campaign progression, explorable structure, and party co-op.
gap_notes: The set lacks the obvious Scott Pilgrim benchmark: Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game would be an expected must-include if available.
The current list leans heavily on arcade-stage beat-'em-ups; the anchor's interconnected-city exploration and quest structure are only strongly reflected by River City Girls 2.
A stronger rail would balance pure genre comps with more progression-forward, semi-open campaign brawlers.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up combat plus leveling, shops, items, quests, multiple fighters, and a connected city to explore rather than only linear stages.

title: Esoteric Ebb

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Esoteric Ebb as an isometric single-player CRPG with deep branching dialogue, abundant choices, open-ended quests, dice-driven TTRPG-style encounters, investigation of a political conspiracy, and exploration of a fantasy city. The strongest identity signals are authored branching narrative, quest-solving, detective-mystery framing, and classic CRPG structure rather than party tactics or action combat. Combat is explicitly de-emphasized ('violence as a last resort'), so western_narrative_rpg fits better than party_based_crpg or combat-forward RPG archetypes.

overall_note: This candidate set is very strong and captures the anchor's dialogue-first, investigation-led CRPG identity well. Disco Elysium is an obvious essential benchmark, with the Torment games as close philosophical/text-heavy CRPG neighbors. Citizen Sleeper and Pentiment are looser but still credible because they share choice-driven investigation and low-combat narrative progression.
anchor_summary: Esoteric Ebb is a single-player isometric narrative CRPG with strong Disco Elysium DNA: investigation-heavy questing, branching dialogue, internal stat/voice interplay, dice-driven checks, failure-forward roleplay, and combat treated as a secondary or last-resort solution.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest on dialogue-first narrative RPGs, but it is somewhat light on other modern isometric CRPGs with explicit dice-check/skill-check quest structure.
Only one candidate really matches the anchor's specific 'stats/voices in your head' and failure-forward detective loop exactly; the rest trend broader narrative RPG or classic text-heavy CRPG.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-perfect comp: isometric detective RPG, internalized skill voices, heavy dialogue checks, political/murder investigation, and failure-forward tabletop-style roleplay over combat.

Strong overlap in dice-driven decisions, faction navigation, authored narrative progression, and low-combat roleplay, but it is structurally more of a narrative survival/life-management RPG than a classic isometric CRPG.
Shares mystery-solving, dialogue-led investigation, and consequential authored choices, but lacks the CRPG stat-check/build identity and tabletop-style systemic roleplaying that define the anchor.

title: iRacing Arcade

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy is strong and concrete: the game is repeatedly described as a 'third-person arcade racer' focused on licensed car racing, online races, global time trials, and weekly events. Core play is competing in races, chasing lap times, and progressing through a motorsport legacy structure by earning resources and investing in team growth, but the identity remains vehicular racing first. The 'easy to learn yet endlessly rewarding' physics, stylized recreations of licensed cars/tracks, and explicit arcade-racer phrasing support arcade_racer over realistic_racer, while the management layer appears secondary rather than transforming it into a sim/tycoon game.

overall_note: This rail has several credible arcade/simcade motorsport neighbors, with New Star GP standing out as the clearest fit because it also combines accessible racing with meaningful team-management progression. The weaker spots are candidates that either skew more toward pure race spectacle without the management loop, or toward exaggerated arcade gimmicks that drift away from the anchor's licensed-motorsport/time-trial identity.
anchor_summary: iRacing Arcade is an accessible motorsport-focused arcade racer built around circuit racing, lap-time chasing, online competition, and a notable meta-progression layer where race earnings feed team/car/staff growth. Best comps should preserve that mix of approachable handling, motorsport structure, and progression beyond one-off races.
gap_notes: No candidate is a true must-keep benchmark or base-game pairing; the set lacks an obvious flagship comp in the exact arcade-motorsport-with-meta-progression niche.

title: Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently depict a third-person single-player action-adventure campaign centered on two protagonists with distinct combat abilities, battling enemies through ruined gothic spaces while solving environmental puzzles and progressing through an authored dark-fantasy story. The text strongly supports melee/supernatural combat, handcrafted linear-to-semi-open level spaces, puzzle gating, and long-form campaign structure, which best fits cinematic_action_adventure. A light action_platformer adjacency is present due to traversal/platform navigation, but platforming does not appear primary.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's story-driven third-person action-adventure identity fairly well, with one especially strong franchise match and two solid genre neighbors. anchor_summary: Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered is a single-player third-person cinematic action-adventure built around melee/supernatural combat, authored dark-fantasy progression, environmental puzzles, traversal/platforming, and linear-to-semi-linear exploration across handcrafted spaces.
gap_notes: The Soul Reaver remaster is the clearest must-keep comp because it shares franchise lineage, protagonist overlap, puzzle structure, and close moment-to-moment gameplay identity.
Darksiders III fits the third-person combat-plus-exploration template, but its more Soulslike challenge model and open-ended structure make it less exact than the best match.

Castlevania: Lords of Shadow is a credible dark-fantasy cinematic action-adventure neighbor, though its combat spectacle and pacing are somewhat more combo-driven and less tightly aligned with Defiance's specific identity.

title: Go! Go! Mister Chickums

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'classic single-screen platformer'/'2D platformer' with '100 lovingly designed screens,' explicit 'jump and run' play, local 2-player couch co-op, and arcade-inspired leaderboard chasing. title: The Abbess Garden

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently describe a single-player, third-person cozy gardening game set in historical France. The core loop is restoring and tending a garden in a free-form natural space, growing and identifying plants through simulated ecosystem factors like soil, moisture, and plant proximity, while progressing gentle quests, uncovering hidden objects, helping NPCs, and pursuing romance. The game is explicitly peaceful and non-combat, with linear story advancement layered onto an at-your-own-pace life/gardening routine. That aligns best with farming_sim, with life_sim as a secondary influence due to social/romance and community-help elements.

overall_note: The rail has one especially close gardening/restoration comp, a few solid cozy-life or restoration neighbors, and one broader genre benchmark that is somewhat less precise. Biggest gap is the lack of more directly comparable peaceful gardening/restoration sims with strong narrative structure and low farm-management overhead.
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player third-person gardening/restoration game centered on free-form plant care in a historically grounded abbey garden. Core play is non-combat tending, identifying plants, uncovering hidden objects, completing gentle quests, unlocking new garden areas, and progressing a light linear story with community interaction and romance.
gap_notes: Need more games centered specifically on gardening or botanical restoration rather than broad farming/life-sim loops.
The anchor is peaceful, linear, and non-combat; candidates with sandbox sprawl, combat, or heavy side systems are looser fits.
Historical/botanical puzzle overlap appears once, but more third-person cozy restoration comps would strengthen the rail.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest match on free-form gardening, restoring an overgrown space, relaxed task-driven progression, unlocking seeds/tools/areas, and a peaceful non-combat structure.

Shares calm botanical play, historical setting, and figuring out ideal growth conditions, but it is fundamentally a first-person environmental puzzle game rather than an ongoing life/gardening sim.

Understandable cozy farming benchmark with restoration, relationships, and crop-growing, but its open-ended farm RPG loop, mining/combat, economy management, and multiplayer profile make it a much broader and less precise match.

title: World of Warcraft: Midnight

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored text focuses almost entirely on setting, zones, and story context for Azeroth/Quel'Thalas/Xal'atath, without concrete gameplay loop, combat, progression, perspective, or mode details. The only direct gameplay-identity signal is a single Metacritic genre label of 'MMORPG', which is not sufficient on its own to confidently map this specific release to an allowed archetype such as mmo_action_rpg, especially since the record is not marked as DLC and therefore cannot inherit World of Warcraft parent evidence under the provided rules.

overall_note: The rail is anchored by the clearest comp in The War Within and then mostly stays within large-scale fantasy/MMO neighbors. The main gap is the absence of the actual World of Warcraft base game/retail shell, and a few neighbors drift farther on combat feel or progression structure.
anchor_summary: World of Warcraft: Midnight is best understood as a modern retail World of Warcraft expansion: a fantasy theme-park MMORPG built around persistent online character progression, class/role-based hotbar combat, questing through new zones, and expansion-driven group/endgame play.
gap_notes: Missing the base retail World of Warcraft game, which would be an expected base-game pairing for a WoW expansion anchor.
The strongest candidates are expansion-to-expansion or theme-park MMORPG comps; weaker entries diverge on combat model and solo-vs-group emphasis.

title: Soulshard

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid: most candidates target side-view co-op-capable platforming with environmental hazards and low combat emphasis. The main spread issue is that several candidates lean more heavily into co-op puzzle design than Soulshard, which seems more like a solo-capable trap-navigation precision platformer with co-op support rather than a fully co-op-defined game.
anchor_summary: Soulshard appears to be a 2D level-based precision/puzzle-platformer centered on trap avoidance, environmental traversal, and optional local co-op, with little evidence of combat or exploration-heavy progression.
gap_notes: Several candidates are more explicitly puzzle-coop or partnership-dependent than the anchor, which may overstate Soulshard's co-op centrality.
The set does well on side-scrolling traversal and hazard navigation, but fewer picks land squarely on minimalist trap-heavy precision platforming.
No candidate is a clearly wrong genre match, though some are looser due to stronger puzzle/gimmick identity than the anchor.

title: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The set has a credible top comp and several understandable neighbors, but it leans broad and misses an especially tight same-identity match. Best candidates emphasize turn-based expedition survival or low-combat discovery; weaker ones drift toward real-time sandbox survival or narrative adventure without the same survival-puzzle loop.
anchor_summary: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition is a single-player turn-based survival/exploration adventure with strong resource pressure, navigation, environmental puzzle solving, crafting/upgrades, and mystery discovery in an alien ocean. Its identity is defined more by deliberate turn-based survival-puzzle progression and low/no combat than by pure action, open-world sandboxing, or narrative-only adventure.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-'same' match for Aquamarine's specific blend of turn-based survival, environmental puzzle solving, and authored mystery progression.
The rail over-indexes on shared exploration/setting and under-serves the game's puzzle-forward, low-combat structure.
A stronger rail would likely include more turn-based survival/adventure hybrids or puzzle-led expedition games rather than open-world real-time survival comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit in the set: turn-based expedition structure, survival/resource management, procedural hazards, and roguelike tension align closely, even if it is more party/event driven and less environmental-puzzle focused.

Shares journey-based survival, supply management, hazard mitigation, and campaign progression, but its wagon-travel/event structure is less exploration-puzzle driven and less discovery-focused than Aquamarine.

Very strong overlap in alien-ocean survival, scavenging, crafting upgrades, wildlife study, and escape-home framing, but the real-time open-world sandbox loop is materially different from Aquamarine's turn-based, puzzle-forward design.

Good match on sci-fi mystery, low combat, deliberate exploration, and puzzle-like discovery, but it is primarily a narrative/language-deciphering adventure rather than a survival-management expedition game.

title: Resident Evil Requiem

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions repeatedly frame the game as 'survival horror' and add concrete loop details: combat, investigations, puzzles, and resource management. The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

overall_note: The rail has strong Resident Evil and survival-horror instincts at the top, but it misses an obvious must-keep benchmark and includes a mislabeled DLC entry that weakens confidence in the set.
anchor_summary: Resident Evil Requiem is positioned as a modern single-player survival-horror campaign built around tense combat, investigations, puzzle-gated progression, resource management, and a mix of first- and third-person play, with one side leaning classic RE survival horror and another leaning more action-horror via Leon.
gap_notes: Resident Evil 2 is the clearest benchmark here and should lead the rail.

Resident Evil Village, Resident Evil 4, and The Evil Within 2 are all plausible neighbors, but they are less exact than Resident Evil 2 because they skew more action-forward, more linear-set-piece-driven, or more semi-open/stealth-oriented.

The entry requested as Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is actually Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe, a DLC expansion with a more specialized hand-to-hand combat profile, so it is not a proper 'same' comp for the anchor.
Given the anchor's classic survival-horror plus investigation framing, the set feels light on exact puzzle-gated, resource-managed survival-horror comps beyond Resident Evil 2.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct gameplay match: modern RE survival horror with tense exploration, scarce resources, puzzle solving, dual campaigns, and Raccoon City structure.
Strong modern RE survival-horror fit with inventory pressure, pursuit, authored progression, and first-person combat, though it leans more action-forward than the anchor's investigative/classic side.
Good survival-horror neighbor with limited ammo, exploration, horror combat, and detective framing, but its semi-open hubs, stealth, and trap crafting make it looser.

Relevant especially for the Leon/action-horror side: over-the-shoulder combat, horror campaign, and some resource pressure, but it is more combat- and set-piece-driven than classic puzzle-gated survival horror.

title: Laysara: Summit Kingdom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong and stays close to city-building and logistics management. Its best candidates capture Laysara’s economy chains, settlement building, and hazard/infrastructure problem-solving. The main gaps are that the set leans either toward roguelite/survival colony formats or much broader nation/empire simulators, while Laysara’s distinctive identity is a focused, combat-free mountain city builder with interconnected settlements and terrain-constrained transport engineering.
anchor_summary: Laysara: Summit Kingdom is a combat-free, single-player city builder centered on economy management, production chains, population needs, transport-network engineering, multi-settlement coordination, and environmental hazard mitigation in difficult mountain terrain. Best matches should share a systemic city-building/logistics core, optimization-heavy play, and survival-through-infrastructure rather than combat, grand strategy, or pure colony character simulation.
gap_notes: No candidate is an exact 'same' match for Laysara’s specific combination of multi-town mountain building, avalanche/weather response, and logistics-first route construction.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

overall_note: The rail mostly targets the anchor's true gameplay identity as a retro-inspired 2D action-platformer rather than the misfired beat-'em-up taxonomy. Best candidates match the anchor's arcade stage-clear platforming, while the weakest entry drifts toward interconnected exploration rather than level-based action-platforming.
anchor_summary: A classic-style 2D side-scrolling action-platformer remake built around level-based progression, precise jumping, enemy hazards, weapons/power-ups, hidden areas, vehicles, and boss encounters in an arcade challenge structure.
gap_notes: No candidate is the original The NewZealand Story or a direct base/legacy pairing, which would be an obvious editorial comp for this remake.
The strongest fits are classic arcade-style action-platformers/remakes; the set becomes looser once it moves into more exploratory or more modern set-piece-driven platformers.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match: retro arcade-origin 2D action-platforming, linear stage progression, precise jumps, projectile combat, hazards, and boss encounters align strongly with the anchor.

title: The Demon Queen's Dire Dilemma

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is strong and direct for a visual novel classification. Multiple store/editorial descriptions explicitly call it a 'visual novel' and describe '60k of text,' '2-4 hours of reading,' and 'a linear narrative with no choices or branches.' The gameplay identity is therefore narrative-reading focused rather than action, strategy, or exploration driven. Supporting facets include single-player mode, cute anime-style presentation, casual genre labeling, and absence of combat mechanics. The premise centers on character interaction between the demon queen and princess, supporting an authored linear interpersonal drama structure within a fantasy setting.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct because all candidates are yuri story-led visual novels centered on reading and character relationships. title: Afterplace

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

overall_note: This rail has a strong grasp of Afterplace's top-down exploration-and-discovery identity, but it leans a bit too far toward harder, more structured combat-adventure neighbors. The best fits are games centered on curiosity, secrets, nonlinear roaming, and light action progression; the weaker fits skew more boss-driven, mechanically demanding, or progression-gated than Afterplace's freer wandering style.
anchor_summary: Afterplace is a single-player, top-down/open-world action-adventure with light RPG layering: freeform exploration, minimal handholding, hidden paths and dungeons, simple hack-and-slash combat, NPC dialogue, quest/exploration progression, equipment upgrades, and choice-shaped endings. Its identity leans more toward discovery-first wandering and authored but optional narrative than toward highly technical combat or tightly gated metroidvania structure.
gap_notes: The set captures top-down exploration well, but several candidates are significantly harsher or more combat-technical than Afterplace.
The rail underrepresents games with strong NPC interaction, loose narrative choice, and low-handholding open wandering as core identity traits.

title: Tales of Berseria Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

overall_note: The set is strong and includes the essential base game plus multiple close Tales-series neighbors that share the same party-based action-JRPG identity. Scarlet Nexus is a reasonable looser comp but sits clearly below the Tales titles because its combat and campaign structure are more action-forward and less like the classic Tales loop.
anchor_summary: Tales of Berseria Remastered is a story-led JRPG/action RPG built around a fixed party, real-time combo-driven Artes combat, character progression, overworld/town/dungeon travel, and a long-form authored campaign. Best matches should preserve the Tales-style party action-JRPG loop rather than just anime presentation or narrative tone.
gap_notes: The original Tales of Berseria is an obvious must-include base-game pairing for a remaster rail.

Scarlet Nexus is acceptable as an adjacent action-JRPG neighbor, but it is notably less similar in party control and overall structure than the Tales candidates.

Very close series neighbor with the same Tales framework of party-based real-time battles, authored progression, and overworld/town/dungeon adventure.
Another clear Tales fit, preserving the party action combat, combo-oriented Artes play, and long-form story JRPG structure.
A solid narrative action RPG with fast combo combat and companion systems, but it is more action-forward and less aligned with the classic Tales party-JRPG loop.

title: Towerborne

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

overall_note: The set captures Towerborne's core identity as a 2D co-op brawler with RPG progression reasonably well. Dragon's Crown Pro is the clearest benchmark, Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong genre neighbor, and Young Souls/River City Girls 2 are valid but somewhat looser due to mode scale or stronger emphasis on story/open exploration over the shared-session loot-brawler loop.
anchor_summary: Towerborne is a side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up/action RPG hybrid: combo-driven lane brawling, up-to-4-player co-op, weapon/build customization, hub-to-expedition structure, and repeatable progression through gear, crafting, and difficulty scaling.
gap_notes: Dragon's Crown Pro is the strongest fit and should anchor the rail.
Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong inclusion, but its progression is lighter and more arcade-leaning than Towerborne's.
Young Souls fits the side-scrolling action-RPG brawler space well, though its co-op scale and campaign emphasis make it a bit less direct than Dragon's Crown Pro or Castle Crashers Remastered.
River City Girls 2 is relevant, but its open-city progression and classic brawler focus make it a looser match for Towerborne's loot-forward co-op expedition loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match on side-scrolling co-op brawler combat, class/gear progression, repeated dungeon runs, and loot-driven action-RPG structure.
Excellent 4-player side-scrolling beat-'em-up comp with stage-based runs, weapon variety, and light character progression, though less loot/build focused.
Modern 2D brawler/action-RPG with gear upgrades, hub returns, and co-op, but more story/campaign driven and less tuned to 4-player repeatable party brawling.
Strong co-op side-scrolling beat-'em-up with leveling, items, and expanded progression, but more open-ended urban brawler than loot-centric co-op action-RPG.
